Title: BANKS/BANKING:  Provides relative to virtual currency. (8/1/26)
Summary: BANKS/BANKING:  Provides relative to virtual currency. (8/1/26)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 287 BY SENATOR REESE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 6:1389(B), 1393.1(E)(6), (F)(4), the introductory paragraph of 3 1393.1(H), and 1393.1(H)(1), (2), (3), and (6) and to enact R.S. 6:1389 (C), (D), and 4 (E) and 1393.1(E)(7), relative to virtual currency; to provide for virtual currency 5 kiosks; to provide for cancellation and refund of transactions; to provide for 6 reporting and disclosures; to provide for terms, conditions, and procedures; and to 7 provide for related matters. 8 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 9 Section 1. R.S. 6:1389(B), 1393.1(E)(6), (F)(4), the introductory paragraph of 10 1393.1(H), and 1393.1(H)(1), (2), (3), and (6) are hereby amended and reenacted and R.S. 11 6:1389(C), (D), and (E) and 1393.1(E)(7) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 12 §1389. Virtual currency kiosks; maximum daily transaction limit; cancellation and 13 refund 14 * * * 15 B.(1) The owner or operator of a virtual currency kiosk shall wait 16 seventy-two hours before processing a user's virtual currency kiosk transaction or 17 shall, at such owner's or operator's cost and within seventy-two hours after a virtual 18 currency kiosk transaction, allow the user to cancel and receive a full refund for the 19 virtual currency kiosk transaction. 20 (2) Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph (1) of this Subsection 21 or any other provision of law to the contrary, the owner or operator of a virtual 22 currency kiosk shall, at the owner or operator's cost, allow the user to cancel ACT No. 482 SB NO. 287 ENROLLED 1 and receive a full refund for a virtual currency kiosk transaction at any time if 2 the owner or operator was not licensed in accordance with the provisions of this 3 Chapter at the time of the transaction. 4 C.(1) A virtual currency kiosk owner or operator shall acknowledge and 5 respond to a cancellation and refund request made pursuant to the provisions 6 of Subsection B of this Section within ten business days. A virtual currency 7 kiosk owner or operator's response shall clearly disclose all requirements for 8 a refund. 9 (2) For a refund request for suspected fraudulent activity, an owner or 10 operator may require a user to provide proof of a police report or other 11 governmental entity report of the suspected fraudulent activity and proof of 12 identification. Submitting either a police report or proof of a complaint 13 submitted to the Internet Crime Complaint Center to the owner or operator of 14 a virtual currency kiosk shall constitute suspected fraudulent activity. 15 (3) Any refund requested pursuant to this Subsection shall be processed 16 and completed either within ninety calendar days from the user's initial request 17 or ninety calendar days from when the user submitted proof of a police or other 18 governmental entity report of the suspected fraudulent activity and proof of 19 identification, if such requirements are part of the owner or operator's refund 20 policy and these requirements were clearly communicated to the user in 21 compliance with Paragraph (1) of this Subsection, whichever is later. 22 D. Each virtual currency kiosk owner or operator shall offer, during the 23 hours of operation of the virtual currency kiosk, live customer support by 24 telephone from a toll-free number prominently displayed on the virtual 25 currency kiosk. The toll-free number shall also be provided on the customer 26 receipt to a transaction as provided in R.S. 6:1393.1(H). 27 E. Each virtual currency kiosk owner or operator shall submit quarterly 28 reports to the commissioner of all cancellation and refund requests made by 29 residents pursuant to the provisions of this Section. The report shall include the 30 name and contact information of the customer who made the request, the SB NO. 287 ENROLLED 1 transaction date, and the amount of the transaction that was requested to be 2 cancelled. 3 * * * 4 §1393.1. Required disclosures 5 * * * 6 E. When opening a new account, and prior to entering into an initial 7 transaction with, for, or on behalf of a resident, each licensee shall disclose in clear, 8 conspicuous, and legible writing in the English language all relevant terms and 9 conditions associated with its virtual currency products, services, and activities and 10 virtual currency generally, including but not limited to the following: 11 * * * 12 (6) The resident's right to contact the office of financial institutions with 13 complaints or questions about the virtual currency kiosk operator's services. 14 The licensee shall include in this disclosure the telephone number for the office 15 of financial institutions. 16 (7) Any other disclosures as are customarily given in connection with the 17 opening of resident accounts. 18 F. Prior to each transaction in virtual currency with, for, or on behalf of a 19 resident, each licensee shall furnish to each such customer or account holder a 20 written disclosure in clear, conspicuous, and legible writing in the English language 21 containing the terms and conditions of the transaction, including but not limited to 22 the following: 23 * * * 24 (4) A notice that once executed, the transaction is not reversible may be 25 cancelled within seventy-two hours and a full refund may be received pursuant 26 to the provisions of R.S. 6:1389(B). 27 * * * 28 H. Upon completion of any transaction, each licensee shall provide to the 29 resident, both in paper and electronic form, a receipt containing all of the 30 following information: SB NO. 287 ENROLLED 1 (1) The name and contact information of the licensee, including but not 2 limited to the licensee's business address and a customer service toll-free 3 telephone number established by the licensee to answer questions, to allow 4 residents to cancel transactions and receive a full refund for a virtual currency 5 kiosk transaction as provided in R.S. 6:1389(B), and to allow residents to register 6 complaints. 7 (2) The type, value, date, and precise time of the transaction and the unique 8 identifier of the virtual currency kiosk operator. 9 (3) The full amount of each fee charged, including but not limited to a fee 10 charged directly or indirectly by the virtual currency kiosk operator or a third 11 party involved in the virtual currency transaction. 12 * * * 13 (6) A statement of the refund policy of the licensee in accordance with the 14 provisions of R.S. 6:1389. 15 * * * PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: IDENTITY DATA:  Provides relative to background checks. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: IDENTITY DATA:  Provides relative to background checks. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 288 BY SENATOR REESE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 15:587(A)(1)(b) and (F)(1), 587.2(B), and 587.7(B)(3), relative 3 to criminal history background checks; to provide for the Louisiana Bureau of 4 Criminal Identification and Information; to provide for the duty to provide certain 5 information to the Louisiana State Board of Private Security Examiners and 6 employers; to provide for the release of expunged criminal history record 7 information under certain circumstances; and to provide for related matters. 8 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 9 Section 1. R.S. 15:587(A)(1)(b) and (F)(1), 587.2(B), and 587.7(B)(3) are hereby 10 amended and reenacted to read as follows: 11 §587. Duty to provide information; processing fees; Louisiana Bureau of Criminal 12 Identification and Information 13 A.(1) * * * 14 (b) The Louisiana State Board of Private Security Examiners shall be entitled 15 to the criminal history record and identification files of the bureau on those persons 16 seeking to be licensed or registered by the board as a means of performing 17 background checks on those individuals. In addition, in order to determine an 18 applicant's eligibility or suitability for licensure or registration under the provisions 19 of the Private Security Regulatory and Licensing Law, each applicant shall be 20 fingerprinted, and the fingerprints shall be forwarded by the bureau to the Federal 21 Bureau of Investigation for a national criminal history record check. 22 * * * 23 F.(1) An employer or his representative shall be entitled to obtain conviction 24 records of an applicant seeking employment, directly from the Bureau of Criminal 25 Identification and Information in order to further qualify the applicant for the 26 position being sought, if the applicant has signed a consent form authorizing the 27 employer to obtain such conviction records. Upon written request and upon ACT No. 483 SB NO. 288 ENROLLED 1 presentation of a consent form prepared by the bureau and executed by the applicant, 2 the bureau shall provide conviction records of the applicant to the employer or his 3 representative in a timely manner. In addition, each applicant shall be fingerprinted, 4 and the fingerprints shall be forwarded by the bureau to the Federal Bureau of 5 Investigation for a national criminal history check or shall submit fingerprints to 6 the bureau. Records obtained pursuant to the provisions of this Subsection shall not 7 include those records which have been ordered expunged pursuant to R.S. 44:9. The 8 provisions of Subsection B of this Section shall apply to any request made pursuant 9 to this Subsection. The bureau shall not be liable civilly or criminally for the release 10 of information released pursuant to the provisions of this Subsection. 11 * * * 12 §587.2. Institutions of postsecondary education; criminal history information 13 * * * 14 B. When a criminal history records check is requested pursuant to Subsection 15 A of this Section, the institution of postsecondary education shall be provided with 16 state or and national criminal history record information, or both, from the Louisiana 17 Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information and from the Federal Bureau of 18 Investigation relative to the applicant prospective employee whose fingerprints have 19 been obtained by the institution pursuant to this Section. The Bureau of Criminal 20 Identification and Information shall, upon request and after receipt of fingerprint 21 cards and other identifying information as required by the bureau from the 22 institution, make available to the institution all prior arrests for any sex offense, 23 aggravated offense, or any sexual offense against a victim who is a minor as defined 24 in R.S. 15:541 or any crime of violence as enumerated in R.S. 14:2(B) and 25 conviction information for any offense contained in the bureau's criminal history 26 record and identification files, which pertains to the applicant or prospective 27 employee, including convictions dismissed pursuant to Code of Criminal Procedure 28 Article 893 or 894. In addition, when the institution of postsecondary education 29 requests such criminal history record information, the bureau shall forward the 30 fingerprints to the Federal Bureau of Investigation for a national criminal history SB NO. 288 ENROLLED 1 check and make such conviction information available to the institution, which 2 pertains to the applicant or prospective employee. 3 * * * 4 §587.7. Volunteer and employee criminal history system 5 * * * 6 B. * * * 7 (3)(a) When a criminal history records check is requested by a qualified 8 entity pursuant to Paragraph (1) of this Subsection, the bureau shall provide the 9 qualified entity with the state criminal history record information of the individual 10 subject to the inquiry. In addition, when the qualified entity requests national 11 criminal history records checks, the bureau shall forward the fingerprints to the 12 Federal Bureau of Investigation for a national criminal history records check and 13 shall provide the qualified entity with the national criminal history record 14 information of the individual subject to the inquiry. 15 (b) The bureau may provide expunged criminal history record 16 information from the state's criminal history record repository to public 17 qualified entities at its discretion based upon strong evidence provided by the 18 public qualified entity that its possessing of the expunged information would be 19 in the best interest to protect the children, the elderly, or individuals with 20 disabilities, served by the public qualified entity. Private qualified entities shall 21 be prohibited from receiving expunged criminal history record information. 22 * * * PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: PUBLIC RECORDS:  Provides for confidentiality of certain information in public records requests for universities. (8/1/26)
Summary: PUBLIC RECORDS:  Provides for confidentiality of certain information in public records requests for universities. (8/1/26)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 289 BY SENATOR ABRAHAM 1 2 AN ACT 3 To enact R.S. 44:3.7 and to repeal R.S. 44:4(56), relative to public records; to provide for 4 exemptions to the public records law for certain university records; to provide for 5 confidentiality of applicants for certain executive-level university positions; to 6 provide for the privacy of university donors; to provide for the confidentiality of 7 research and proprietary information at universities; and to provide for related 8 matters. 9 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 10 Section 1. R.S. 44:3.7 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 11 §3.7. Certain records of public postsecondary education institutions 12 A. Nothing in this Chapter shall be construed to require the disclosure 13 of records, or the information contained therein, held by a public postsecondary 14 education institution, which records are: 15 (1) Applications, resumes, or the personally identifiable information of 16 an applicant for president, chancellor, senior vice chancellor, or athletic coach 17 at the public postsecondary education institution, unless the applicant has 18 authorized the disclosure of such records and information. This exemption shall 19 expire once an applicant is approved for the position by the governing board, 20 at which time the records of the top three finalists, including the approved 21 applicant, shall become public. This exemption shall continue for any applicant 22 that is not among the top three finalists for the position, unless the applicant has 23 authorized the disclosure of such records and information. 24 (2) Any portion of a record that identifies the name of a donor to the ACT No. 596 SB NO. 289 ENROLLED 1 public postsecondary education institution or its affiliated foundation if the 2 donor has requested that his name remain confidential. 3 (3) Records that contain proprietary, unpublished, or incomplete 4 academic research and unsubmitted grant applications. This exception shall 5 expire upon the disclosure, publication, public announcement, or public 6 knowledge of the research or the grant application, upon the submission of the 7 related grant application, or upon the grant or denial of related intellectual 8 property rights. 9 (4)(a) Records of negotiations for industry partnerships with the 10 institution upon a determination by the custodian of records that disclosure of 11 the information would have a detrimental effect on the negotiations. The 12 custodian's determination shall be disclosed in response to a request for the 13 records. 14 (b) No information made confidential pursuant to this Paragraph shall 15 remain confidential for more than twelve months from the date of the 16 custodian's determination that disclosure would be detrimental; however, if the 17 negotiation remains active and the custodian makes a new determination that 18 the disclosure of the information would be detrimental to the negotiations, such 19 information shall remain confidential while the negotiation remains active, not 20 to exceed an additional six months. 21 B. This Chapter shall not apply to the personally identifiable information 22 of any person who reports a violation of a student code of conduct or other 23 policy intended for the safety of students or employees of a postsecondary 24 education institution, personally identifiable information of any reported 25 witness to the reported violation, and, if the reported violation involves violence 26 or abuse, personally identifiable information of any person who may be a victim 27 of violence or abuse directly related to the reported violation if such information 28 is in the custody or control of the postsecondary education institution or 29 management board and was received in accordance with adopted, written 30 policies applicable to the postsecondary education institution and the students SB NO. 289 ENROLLED 1 and employees of the postsecondary education institution, unless access to the 2 information is specifically required by other provisions of law of this state or by 3 federal law or is ordered by a court under rules of discovery. 4 Section 2. R.S. 44:4(56) is hereby repealed. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 290 BY SENATOR ABRAHAM 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 17:3911(C)(5), relative to the data collection system; to provide 3 relative to the process required for the state Department of Education to change 4 certain information assigned to a student; to provide for an effective date; and to 5 provide for related matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. R.S. 17:3911(C)(5) is hereby amended and reenacted to read as follows: 8 §3911. Data collection system; establishment 9 * * * 10 C. The department shall: 11 * * * 12 (5) Obtain concurrence of the city, parish, or other local public school system 13 prior to changing the assigning an exit code to a student that differs from the exit 14 code assigned to a student by the school system. 15 * * * 16 Section 2. Title: ARCHITECTS:  Provides relative to the practice of architecture. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: ARCHITECTS:  Provides relative to the practice of architecture. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 291 BY SENATOR ABRAHAM 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 37:141 through 154, 156, 157, and 158 and to repeal R.S. 3 37:146.1, relative to architects; to provide for licensing of entities engaged in 4 architecture; to provide for composition of the State Board of Architectural 5 Examiners; to provide for the powers, duties, and functions of the board; to provide 6 for licensing requirements; to provide for initial licenses and qualifications; to 7 provide for issuance of reciprocal licenses; to provide for board approval to revoke, 8 rescind, and suspend licenses; to provide for violations and enforcement of board 9 actions; and to provide for related matters. 10 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 11 Section 1. R.S. 37:141 through 154, 156, 157, and 158 are hereby amended and 12 reenacted to read as follows: 13 §141. Policy and definitions 14 A. In order The legislature finds that the practice of architecture by 15 qualified individuals with professional expertise is necessary to safeguard life, 16 health, and property and to promote the public welfare, the practice of architecture 17 in this state is reserved to those persons who have the proper qualifications and have 18 been registered by the board. 19 B. The legislature further finds that the practice of architecture by 20 individuals and firms should be regulated in the public interest. 21 C. Therefore, it is the legislative intent and the purpose of this Chapter 22 to promote, preserve, and protect the public health, safety, and welfare by 23 regulating the practice of architecture and ensuring that any individual or firm 24 practicing or offering to practice architecture shall be registered with and 25 licensed by the board before engaging in the practice. In furtherance of this 26 purpose, this Chapter creates the State Board of Architectural Examiners 27 whose members, authority, functions, and procedures shall be established in ACT No. 484 SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 accordance with the provisions of this Chapter. This Chapter shall be liberally 2 construed to carry out these objectives and purposes. 3 D. As used in this Chapter: 4 (1) "Applicant" means an individual who seeks a license in accordance 5 with the process set forth by the board. 6 (2) "Approved educational program" means an educational program for 7 architecture that is accepted by the board. 8 (3) "Approved examination" means an examination for architecture that 9 is accepted by the board. 10 (4) "Approved experience program" means an experience program for 11 architecture that is accepted by the board. 12 (5) "Architect" means a person who is technically and legally qualified to 13 practice architecture an individual currently licensed by the board who 14 demonstrates competence to engage in the practice of architecture as provided 15 in this Chapter. 16 (2)(6) "Board" means the State Board of Architectural Examiners. 17 (7) "Competence" means the knowledge, experience, and skill required 18 by an architect for the practice of architecture in accordance with the accepted 19 standard of care. 20 (8) "Firm" means any legally formed business entity registered with the 21 board through which architectural services are provided. 22 (9) "Jurisdiction" means any state, commonwealth, the District of 23 Columbia, or other insular territory of the United States. 24 (10) "License" means an approval granted by the board to an individual 25 or a firm to engage in the practice of architecture. 26 (11) "Licensing authority" means the public board, agency, or body 27 which has been authorized by a jurisdiction's governing authority to grant 28 licenses and regulate the practice of architecture in that jurisdiction. 29 (12) "NCARB" means the National Council of Architectural 30 Registration Boards. SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 (13) "Person" means any individual, firm, partnership, association, joint 2 venture, cooperative, corporation, or other group or combination acting in 3 concert. 4 (3)(14) The "practice of architecture" is the rendering or offering of the 5 services specified in this Paragraph in connection with the design, construction, 6 enlargement, or alteration of a building, a group of buildings, or the space within and 7 surrounding buildings which have human occupancy or habitation as their principal 8 purpose. Such services shall include the following: planning; providing preliminary 9 studies, designs, drawings, specifications, and other technical submissions; 10 administration of construction contracts; and the coordination of any element of 11 technical submissions prepared by others, including but not limited to engineers and 12 landscape architects, as appropriate. The practice of architecture shall not include the 13 practice of engineering as defined in R.S. 37:682; however, a registered architect 14 may perform such engineering work as is incidental to the practice of architecture. 15 (15) "Responsible control" means responsibility for exercising the 16 ultimate authority over, and possessing the knowledge and ability to oversee, 17 delegate, and integrate the design and technical decisions related to the 18 preparation of the project's instruments of service and the project's 19 implementation in conformance with the standard of care. 20 (16) "Technical submissions" means the documents necessary to 21 demonstrate compliance with applicable regulatory requirements or to 22 fabricate or construct a project including but not limited to drawings, 23 specifications, performance criteria, and installation requirements. 24 C.E. The definition of the practice of architecture set forth in Paragraph B(3) 25 (D)(14) of this Section may include, but shall not be construed as precluding 26 nonlicensed persons from performing the following services: project development;, 27 feasibility studies;, planning;, energy consumption analysis;, and interior design. 28 §142. Board appointed by governor; removal 29 A.(1) The State Board of Architectural Examiners is hereby created within 30 the office of the governor and shall consist of seven members, five of whom shall be SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 architects selected by the governor as provided for in Subsection B of this Section, 2 one selected by the governor as provided for in Subsection C of this Section, and 3 one selected by the governor as provided for in Subsection D of this Section. All 4 nominees and appointees shall be domiciled in the state of Louisiana. The five 5 architect members shall be appointed, one each, from the five districts provided for 6 in Paragraph (2) of this Subsection. The governor has the right to remove any or all 7 members of the board for inefficiency or neglect of duty. 8 (2) The districts shall be composed as follows: 9 (a) District 1: Orleans, Plaquemines, and St. Bernard. 10 (b) District 2: Assumption, Jefferson, Lafourche, St. Charles, St. James, St. 11 John the Baptist, St. Tammany, Terrebonne, and Washington. 12 (c) District 3: Ascension, East Baton Rouge, East Feliciana, Iberville, 13 Livingston, St. Helena, St. Martin, Tangipahoa, West Baton Rouge, and West 14 Feliciana. 15 (d) District 4: Acadia, Allen, Avoyelles, Beauregard, Calcasieu, Cameron, 16 Evangeline, Grant, Iberia, Jefferson Davis, Lafayette, Natchitoches, Pointe Coupee, 17 Rapides, Sabine, St. Landry, St. Mary, Vermilion, and Vernon. 18 (e) District 5: Bienville, Bossier, Caddo, Caldwell, Catahoula, Claiborne, 19 Concordia, DeSoto, East Carroll, Franklin, Jackson, LaSalle, Lincoln, Madison, 20 Morehouse, Ouachita, Red River, Richland, Tensas, Union, Webster, West Carroll, 21 and Winn. 22 (3) Changes by the legislature in the composition of any of the districts 23 provided for in Paragraph (2) of this Subsection made after an appointment of 24 a member to the board shall not affect an appointment in any way, and any 25 member lawfully appointed prior to such changes shall be allowed to serve the 26 full term for which the member was appointed. 27 B.(1) The five architect members shall be and shall remain domiciled in this 28 state and in the district from which he the member is nominated and appointed. 29 Each shall be a licensed architect and shall have practiced architecture for at least 30 seven years. Each architect member shall be appointed by the governor from a list SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 of three no more than five nominees elected from each of the five districts. An 2 eligible architect desiring nomination shall write a letter to the board so stating. 3 If letters from five or fewer eligible architects are received, no election shall be 4 held, and the board shall submit the names of all eligible architects who have 5 sought nomination from the governor for consideration. If letters from more 6 than five eligible architects are received, an election conducted by the board 7 shall be held to determine the five eligible architects to be included on the list 8 of nominees. The board shall not adopt any rule or regulation which imposes further 9 eligibility requirements for membership on the board. 10 (2) In order to determine the list of nominees for the architect members, there 11 shall be a statewide election to elect three no more than five nominees from each 12 district. Any licensed architect who wishes to be a candidate and have his name 13 placed on the ballot shall send a letter by certified mail to the director of the board 14 indicating his intent to be a candidate. The letter shall be accompanied by a 15 curriculum vitae and shall certify that, if elected, the architect will serve. 16 (3) The election for nominees shall be by either mail ballot or online voting, 17 and only one architect shall be a candidate for nomination from the same 18 architectural firm, corporation, agency, partnership, or other entity. After the 19 election, the three five candidates from each district who received the highest 20 numbers of votes shall have their names submitted to the governor as nominees. All 21 licensed architects residing in this state shall be eligible to vote in the election. 22 (4) The initial and subsequent elections shall be to fill vacancies as they 23 occur. The elections shall be completed by the board within ninety days of the 24 effective vacancy. 25 C. The governor shall appoint one member of the board who shall be an 26 architect who for seven years prior to appointment has been employed full time in 27 architectural education, or who for seven years prior to appointment, has been an 28 administrator of building design, construction, or design standards for government 29 at the local, state, or national level. 30 D. The governor shall appoint one public member of the board who shall be SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 a citizen of the United States who is not actively engaged in or retired from the 2 professions of architecture, engineering, interior design, or landscape architecture, 3 or the occupation of a contractor, or the design or construction of buildings. 4 E. Each board member shall at all times maintain eligibility to serve on 5 the board by avoiding relationships that would interfere with the board's 6 mission of public protection. 7 F. No board member shall be an officer or hold any leadership position 8 in this state's professional association or the national professional association 9 serving architects for the term of the board member's appointment to the 10 board. 11 G. Any vacancy that occurs in the board membership for any reason, 12 including expiration of a term, removal, resignation, death, disability, or 13 disqualification, shall be filled as prescribed in this Section in a timely manner 14 so that the board may fulfill its duties as charged. 15 H. The board may recommend removal of a board member to the 16 governor upon an affirmative vote of a majority of members otherwise eligible 17 to vote upon one or more of the following grounds: 18 (1) The refusal or inability for any reason of a board member to perform 19 the duties required of a board member in an efficient, responsible, and 20 professional manner. 21 (2) The misuse of a board member's position to obtain, or attempt to 22 obtain, any financial or material gain, or any advantage personally or for 23 another, through the office. 24 (3) A final adjudication by a recognized body, including any court, that 25 there has been a violation of the laws governing the practice of architecture by 26 the board member. 27 (4) Conviction of a felony or misdemeanor other than a minor traffic 28 offense. 29 §143. Term of office 30 A. Each member shall serve six years, or until his successor is duly SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 appointed, except the initial educational or regulatory member appointed pursuant 2 to R.S. 37:142(C) shall serve a term of four years and the initial public member 3 appointed pursuant to R.S. 37:142(D) shall serve a term of five years and 4 confirmed. 5 B. In the event of the death or resignation of any board member, the 6 appointment by the governor of his successor shall be only for the remainder 7 of the unexpired term. 8 C. Each appointment by the governor shall be submitted to the Senate 9 for confirmation. 10 D. The board shall advise the governor of the expiration of the term of the 11 board members. All vacancies shall be filled in the same manner as prescribed for 12 new members in R.S. 37:142. 13 §144. Powers, authority, and domicile of board 14 A. Four members of the board constitute a quorum for the purpose of holding 15 examinations, meetings and hearings, adjudicating, granting certificates, and 16 renewing licenses, engaging in rule making, and transacting other business within 17 the scope of this Chapter. 18 B. The board shall elect from its members a president, secretary, and 19 other officers as it deems appropriate and necessary to conduct its business. The 20 president shall preside at all meetings of the board, sign all certificates of 21 licensure, and perform those duties customarily associated with the position and 22 any other duties assigned from by the board. The president may establish board 23 committees as appropriate and necessary for the furtherance of board business 24 and may designate or remove board members as committee members. 25 C. The secretary shall be the official custodian of the records of the 26 board, sign minutes of the board meetings, sign the president's certificates of 27 licensure, and perform the duties of the president when the president is unable 28 to do so. 29 D. The board may delegate authority to its executive director as is 30 customary and necessary to properly fulfill the board's duties. The executive SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 director shall perform such administrative and ministerial duties as the board 2 authorizes. 3 E. The certificate of issuance of a license by the board to an individual or 4 a firm shall entitle the holder to practice as an architect architecture in Louisiana. 5 C.F. The board shall have the power powers and duties as may be 6 necessary to carry out the purposes and enforce any provision of this Chapter 7 and any rule promulgated by the board, including granting and renewing 8 licenses, regulating individuals and firms engaged in the practice of 9 architecture, establishing by rule the requirements and procedures for use of 10 the title "Architect Emeritus". The board shall also have the power to adopt and 11 amend such rules and regulations as are reasonably necessary for the proper 12 performance of its duties, for carrying out the purposes of this Chapter, for 13 continuing education, for the regulation of the proceedings before it, and for the 14 regulation of the practice of architecture under the laws of this state. 15 D.G. The procedure for the adoption, amendment, and repeal of any rule or 16 regulation shall be in accordance with the Administrative Procedure Act, R.S. 49:950 17 et seq., and the rules and regulations which may be adopted by the board shall be 18 available for public inspection at the office of the State Board of Architectural 19 Examiners. 20 E.H. The board may employ executive, clerical, or other and office assistants 21 as are necessary, including an executive director, for the proper performance of its 22 work; may make expenditures from its funds for any purpose which is reasonably 23 necessary for the proper performance of its duties under this Chapter; and may, if 24 necessary, or upon advice of the attorney general, hire counsel for advice, for the 25 representation of the board against violators of this Chapter and for the defense of 26 any action brought against the board or its members or staff. 27 F.I. The domicile of the board shall be Baton Rouge, Louisiana. 28 J. The board, its members, and its agents shall be immune from personal 29 liability for actions taken in good faith in the discharge of the board's 30 responsibilities. The state shall hold the board, its members, and its agents SB NO. 291 ENROLLED 1 harmless from all costs, damages, and attorney fees arising from claims and 2 suits against them with respect to matters to which the immunity applies. 3 G.K.(1) The board may allocate up to ten percent of all license renewal and 4 delinquent fees each fiscal year to a fund to be named the Louisiana Architecture 5 Education and Research Fund. R.S. 37:146.1 is hereby repealed. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: GOVERNOR:  Provides for the office and duties of the office of the state inspector general. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX)
Summary: GOVERNOR:  Provides for the office and duties of the office of the state inspector general. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 292 BY SENATOR STINE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 49:220.21, 220.23, and 220.24 and to repeal R.S. 49:220.26, 3 relative to the powers and duties of the office of the state inspector general; to 4 provide for an effective date; and to provide for related matters. 5 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 6 Section 1. R.S. 49:220.21, 220.23, and 220.24 are hereby amended and reenacted to 7 read as follows: 8 §220.21. Office of the state inspector general; policy and purpose 9 A. The prevention and detection of waste, inefficiencies, mismanagement, 10 misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption in all departments, offices, agencies, 11 boards, commissions, task forces, authorities, and divisions of the executive branch 12 of state government as specifically provided in Title 36 of the Louisiana Revised 13 Statutes of 1950, all hereinafter referred to in this Part collectively as "covered 14 agencies" and individually as "covered agency", is an important responsibility of the 15 state. The efficient and optimal execution of legislative will is an important 16 responsibility of the executive branch. The executive branch is compelled to find 17 efficiency and optimization, to detect waste, inefficiency, and mismanagement, 18 and to prevent misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption in all departments, 19 offices, agencies, boards, commissions, task forces, authorities, and divisions of 20 the executive branch of state government as specifically provided in Title 36 of 21 the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950, referred to in this Part collectively as ACT No. 417 SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 "covered agencies" and individually as "covered agency". 2 B. In view of the responsibility of the state, it is the purpose of this Part to 3 establish an independent office of the state inspector general in the office of the 4 governor to examine and investigate the management and affairs of covered 5 agencies. 6 * * * 7 §220.23. State inspector general and chief integrity officer; appointment; term; 8 vacancy; compensation; removal 9 A.(1) There shall be a state inspector general, hereinafter referred to as the 10 "inspector general" and "chief integrity officer", who shall be appointed by the 11 governor with the consent of the Senate. No person appointed inspector general shall 12 hold or be a candidate for any elective office, including elective political party office, 13 or any other public office or political party office. No person shall be appointed 14 inspector general who has held any elective office or political party office within two 15 years immediately preceding his appointment. No former inspector general shall be 16 eligible to qualify as a candidate for any elective office, including elective political 17 party office, nor shall he assume any elective office or political party office within 18 four years after the termination of his service as inspector general. 19 (2) If a vacancy exists in the office of the state inspector general for more 20 than six months, then the holder of the next highest level administrative position in 21 the office shall become the inspector general, subject to confirmation by the Senate. 22 (3) Not later than one year from the date of appointment, if not already 23 certified, the inspector general shall obtain certification as a Certified Inspector 24 General from the Association of Inspectors General. 25 B. The inspector general shall serve a six-year term. 26 C. The salary of the inspector general shall be fixed by the governor, which 27 amount shall not exceed the amount approved for such position by the legislature 28 while in session. The salary of the inspector general may not be reduced by the 29 governor or the legislature during his term of office. 30 D.(1) Notwithstanding the provisions of Subsection B of this Section, the SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 inspector general may be removed by the governor provided such removal is 2 approved by a majority vote of each house of the legislature. 3 (2) In order to obtain the consent of a majority of the elected members of 4 each house of the legislature, the clerk of the House of Representatives and the 5 secretary of the Senate shall prepare and transmit a ballot to each member of the 6 legislature by certified mail with return receipt requested, unless it is determined that 7 the legislature will be in session in time for the ballots to be distributed to them and 8 returned by them during the session. The ballot shall be uniform and include 9 pertinent information as the clerk and secretary shall determine. 10 §220.24. Authority; duties; powers; standards; functions 11 A. The inspector general shall serve as the executive head and chief 12 administrative officer of the office and shall have responsibility for the policies of 13 the office, except as otherwise provided by this Part, and for the administration, 14 control, and operation of the functions and affairs of the office. 15 B. The inspector general is authorized to examine and investigate the 16 management and affairs of the covered agencies concerning waste, inefficiencies, 17 mismanagement, misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption, to find efficiency and 18 optimization, to detect waste, inefficiency, and mismanagement, and to prevent 19 misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption; and he may conduct all necessary 20 investigations into such areas, including but not limited to: for the purpose of: 21 (1) Misuse of state-owned automobiles, planes, watercraft, and all other 22 movable and immovable property. 23 (2) Evidence of a pattern of excessive bills on state contracts. 24 (3) Unauthorized use of leave. 25 (4) Mismanagement of governmental operations. 26 (5) Waste or abuse of things of value belonging to or used by the covered 27 agencies. 28 (6) Construction, operation, and maintenance of facilities. 29 (1) Eliminating wasteful spending. 30 (2) Improving government efficiency. SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 (3) Optimizing and modernizing government operations. 2 C.(1) The inspector general shall help prevent waste, inefficiency, 3 mismanagement, misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption in covered agencies by 4 periodically reviewing policies and procedures and monitoring operations and 5 making recommendations for improvement. 6 (2) The inspector general shall receive complaints of waste, inefficiency, 7 mismanagement, misconduct, abuse, fraud, or corruption in covered agencies and 8 determine whether they warrant investigation by the inspector general or by 9 appropriate federal, state, or local agencies or may conduct such investigations upon 10 his own initiative. In order to accomplish this, the office of the inspector general 11 shall maintain a toll-free fraud hotline number and web site website for anonymous 12 reporting complaints. 13 (3) The inspector general shall investigate complaints of waste, inefficiencies, 14 mismanagement, misconduct, abuse, fraud, and corruption and, when appropriate, 15 recommend whether disciplinary action or further investigation by appropriate 16 federal, state, or local agencies is warranted and take further action as appropriate. 17 (4) The inspector general shall report complaints of fraud, abuse, or 18 corruption to such federal, state, or local agencies when there is evidence of what 19 may be criminal activity and when otherwise appropriate and shall otherwise 20 cooperate with such agencies in any further action. 21 (5) The inspector general shall make reports of his findings to the governor. 22 Such reports shall be subject to the provisions of R.S. 44:1 et seq. 23 (6) The inspector general shall submit an annual report to the governor and 24 the Joint Legislative Committee on the Budget at the end of each fiscal year that 25 describes the accomplishments and contributions made by the office toward 26 achieving the mission of helping to prevent and detect waste, fraud, and abuse in 27 Louisiana government. Upon completion, as indicated by signature of the inspector 28 general, all final reports of the inspector general immediately shall be filed with the 29 Joint Legislative Committee on the Budget and shall include the response of the 30 agency, if any. All such reports shall be provided to the staff of the governor's office SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 and the Joint Legislative Committee on the Budget upon request. 2 D. The inspector general shall provide for an opportunity for agency response 3 to respond prior to the release of a report unless the inspector general, in conjunction 4 with a United States attorney, the state attorney general, district attorneys, or other 5 prosecutorial agencies, determines that supplying the affected person or entity with 6 such report will jeopardize a pending or potential criminal investigation. (1) The 7 inspector general shall submit an annual report to the governor and the Joint 8 Legislative Committee on the Budget at the end of each fiscal year that 9 describes the accomplishments and contributions made by the office toward 10 achieving the mission of helping to prevent and detect waste, fraud, and abuse 11 in Louisiana government. 12 (2) The inspector general shall make reports of his findings to the 13 governor. Such reports shall be subject to the provisions of R.S. 44:1 et seq. The 14 inspector general shall provide for an opportunity for a covered agency to 15 respond prior to release of a report unless the inspector general, in consultation 16 with a United States attorney, the state attorney general, district attorney, or 17 other prosecutorial agencies, determines that supplying the affected person or 18 covered agency with such report will jeopardize a pending or potential criminal 19 investigation. Upon completion, as indicated by the signature of the inspector 20 general, all final reports of the inspector general shall be filed immediately with 21 the Joint Legislative Committee on the Budget and shall include the response 22 of the covered agency, if any. 23 E. All officers and employees of covered agencies shall extend full 24 cooperation and all reasonable assistance to the inspector general. 25 F.(1) In the performance of his duties, the inspector general and any member 26 of his staff designated by him may seek and obtain sworn testimony from any person 27 using the same procedure as is provided for taking depositions provided for in 28 Article 1443 in the Code of Civil Procedure Article 1443. 29 (2) In the performance of his duties, the inspector general or any member of 30 his staff designated by him may compel the attendance of witnesses to be deposed SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 under oath or the production of public and private records by issuing a subpoena. 2 However, such a subpoena or subpoena duces tecum shall be issued only upon 3 approval of a judge of the district court of the parish in which the office of inspector 4 general is domiciled upon application in writing by the inspector general. The judge 5 shall issue a written decision within seventy-two hours after receipt of such 6 application. Any subpoena for production of private records shall be in compliance 7 with all applicable constitutionally established rights and processes. The subpoena 8 may be served by certified mail, return receipt requested, at the addressee's residence 9 or business address, or by representatives appointed by the inspector general, or may 10 be directed for service to the office of the state police. If a person refuses to obey a 11 subpoena issued by the inspector general, upon application by the inspector general, 12 the district court of the parish in which the office of inspector general is domiciled 13 may issue an order to the person requiring the person to appear before the court to 14 show cause why an order shall not be issued ordering such person to obey the 15 subpoena, and the person may be adjudged in contempt of court. 16 (3)G. The inspector general shall have access to all records, information, 17 data, reports, plans, projections, matters, contracts, memoranda, correspondence, and 18 any other materials of a covered agency and shall be deemed to be an authorized 19 representative and agent of each covered agency for the purposes of: 20 (a)(1) Examining and investigating the records of all contractors, 21 subcontractors, grantees, or subgrantees of covered agencies, which records relate 22 to contracts, subcontracts, grants, or subgrants with a covered agency. 23 (b)(2) Obtaining access to any records of a covered agency in the possession 24 of a third party, including but not limited to bank account records. 25 G.H. The inspector general and employees of the inspector general shall 26 carry identifying cards. 27 H.I. The office of the state inspector general shall adhere to professional 28 standards for initiating and conducting audits, investigations, inspections, and 29 reviews such as those promulgated by the Association of Inspectors General. The 30 office shall develop an operations manual that contains such standards and shall SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 make it available to the public. 2 I.J. The inspector general shall engage in prevention activities, including but 3 not limited to reviewing legislation, rules, regulations, policies, procedures, and 4 transactions; providing for training and education; and making recommendations to 5 the governor and the legislature to strengthen public integrity laws. 6 J.K. The office of the state inspector general is hereby designated as a law 7 enforcement agency and conferred all investigative powers and privileges 8 appurtenant to a law enforcement agency under state law as necessary and in 9 furtherance of the authority, duties, powers, and functions set forth in this Part. These 10 powers and privileges shall not include arrest powers but shall include access to 11 computer systems, information maintained for the use of law enforcement personnel, 12 and any information contained in the criminal history record and identification file 13 of the Louisiana Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information. These powers 14 shall not include arrest powers. 15 K.L. Upon credible information of corruption or fraud, the office of the state 16 inspector general shall notify the appropriate law enforcement agencies. Subsequent 17 to notifying the appropriate law enforcement agency, the inspector general may assist 18 the law enforcement agency in conducting the investigation. Upon detecting a 19 violation of one of the provisions of the Code of Governmental Ethics, the office of 20 the state inspector general may file a complaint with the Board of Ethics. 21 L.M. The office of the state inspector general may conduct joint 22 investigations and projects with other oversight or law enforcement agencies. 23 M.N. In order to fulfill the requirements of this Part, the inspector 24 general shall have the authority to contract for such professional services, 25 without public bid, as are reasonably necessary. 26 O. The inspector general shall do all things necessary to carry out the 27 functions set forth in this Part. 28 Section 2. R.S. 49:220.26 is hereby repealed in its entirety. 29 Section 3. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 30 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature SB NO. 292 ENROLLED 1 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 2 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 3 effective on the day following such approval. Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 294 BY SENATOR TALBOT 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 27:44(15), 205(16), and 353(9), relative to promotional play in 3 certain forms of gaming; to provide for the allowable amount of promotional play 4 deduction in certain forms of gaming; to provide for an effective date; and to provide 5 for related matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. Title: HEALTH/ACC INSURANCE:  Provides for insurance coverage of services for persons with acquired brain injuries. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: HEALTH/ACC INSURANCE:  Provides for insurance coverage of services for persons with acquired brain injuries. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 295 BY SENATOR WHEAT AND REPRESENTATIVE KNOX 1 2 AN ACT 3 To enact R.S. 22:1042.1, relative to coverage of medically necessary treatment for persons 4 with acquired brain injuries; to require health insurance coverage of medically 5 necessary treatment for persons with acquired brain injuries; to provide relative to 6 limitations and cost-sharing; to provide for definitions; to provide for exceptions; to 7 provide for implementation; to provide for applicability; and to provide for related 8 matters. 9 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 10 Section 1. R.S. 22:1042.1 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 11 §1042.1. Coverage for medically necessary treatment for persons with an 12 acquired brain injury 13 A. Every health coverage plan delivered or issued for delivery in this 14 state shall provide, to the extent permitted by federal law, coverage for 15 medically necessary treatment related to or as a result of an acquired brain 16 injury. 17 B.(1) The coverage provided pursuant to this Section shall not include 18 any lifetime limitation or unreasonable annual limitation of the number of days 19 or sessions of treatment services. 20 (2) Any limitations on rehabilitation services in an inpatient 21 rehabilitation facility shall be separate from and shall not be included in any 22 limitations of post-acute rehabilitation. 23 (3) Any limitations shall be separately stated by the health coverage plan 24 issuer. 25 C. The coverage provided pursuant to this Section shall not be subject 26 to any greater deductible, coinsurance, copayments, or out-of-pocket limits than 27 any other similar benefit provided by the health coverage plan. ACT No. 754 SB NO. 295 ENROLLED 1 D. A health coverage plan shall not deny coverage for medically 2 necessary treatment based solely on the location or setting in which the 3 treatment is provided, if the treatment is provided in a setting that is clinically 4 appropriate for the level of care necessary and in compliance with applicable 5 state licensure, certification, registration, or accreditation requirements. 6 Clinically appropriate settings may include but are not limited to inpatient 7 hospital or rehabilitation facility settings, outpatient and day treatment settings, 8 post-acute residential treatment settings, and home- and community-based 9 settings. 10 E. For any medically necessary treatment covered pursuant to this 11 Section, any adverse determination issued in connection with a preauthorization 12 or utilization review shall be made by a clinical peer reviewer. 13 F. The commissioner shall promulgate rules and regulations, in 14 accordance with the Administrative Procedure Act, to establish a process for an 15 expedited appeal of any adverse determination by a health coverage plan issuer 16 for medically necessary treatment covered pursuant to this Section. 17 G. Medically necessary treatment covered pursuant to this Section shall 18 be provided by individual practitioners and treatment facilities qualified to 19 provide acute care and post-acute care rehabilitation services to a person with 20 an acquired brain injury through possession of the appropriate licenses, 21 accreditation, training, and experience deemed customary and routine in the 22 trade practice. 23 H. For the purposes of this Section, the following definitions apply: 24 (1) "Acquired brain injury" or "ABI" means any injury to the brain 25 which is not a progressive neurologic disorder, hereditary, congenital, or 26 degenerative in nature, and which occurs after birth and may be caused by 27 infectious diseases, metabolic disorders, endocrine disorders, diminished 28 oxygen, brain tumors, toxins, a disease that affects the blood supply to the brain, 29 stroke, or a traumatic brain injury. 30 (2) "Adverse determination" means a determination by a clinical peer SB NO. 295 ENROLLED 1 reviewer, upon review based on the clinical information provided, that an 2 admission, extension of stay, or any other covered ABI service is not medically 3 necessary. 4 (3) "Clinical peer reviewer" means a healthcare professional who has 5 experience in the delivery of treatments and services for persons with acquired 6 brain injuries and possesses a current and valid license, certificate, or 7 registration to provide these treatments and services or, where no provision for 8 a license, certificate or registration exists, is credentialed by the national 9 accrediting body appropriate to the profession. 10 (4) "Cognitive communication therapy" means treatment for problems 11 with communication which have an underlying cause in one or more cognitive 12 deficits rather than a primary language or speech deficit. 13 (5) "Cognitive rehabilitation therapy" means a process of relearning 14 cognitive skills essential for daily living through the coordinated specialized, 15 integrated therapeutic treatments which are provided in dynamic settings 16 designed for efficient and effective relearning following damage to brain cells 17 or brain chemistry due to brain injury. 18 (6) "Community reintegration services" means incremental, guided, 19 real-world therapeutic training to develop skills essential for an individual to 20 participate in life including functional daily activities; to safely live 21 independently; and to participate in the community while avoiding 22 rehospitalization and long-term support needs. 23 (7) "Functional rehabilitation therapy" means a structured approach 24 which emphasizes learning by doing, and focuses relearning a specific task in 25 a prescribed format, with maximum opportunity for repeated correct practice. 26 Functional rehabilitation therapy includes compensatory strategies developed 27 for those skills which are persistently impaired with individuals trained on daily 28 implementation. To ensure acquisition and use, the focus of functional 29 rehabilitation therapy is set on relearning those skills essential for safe daily 30 living in home- and community-based settings. SB NO. 295 ENROLLED 1 (8) "Health coverage plan" means any hospital, health, or medical 2 expense insurance policy, hospital or medical service contract, employee welfare 3 benefit plan, contract, or other agreement with a health maintenance 4 organization or a preferred provider organization, health and accident 5 insurance policy, or any other insurance contract of this type in this state, 6 including a group insurance plan or a self-insurance plan. "Health coverage 7 plan" does not include a plan providing coverage for excepted benefits as 8 defined in R.S. 22:1061, limited benefit health insurance plans, short-term 9 policies that have a term of less than twelve months, Medicare, Medicaid, and 10 the Office of Group Benefits programs. 11 (9) "Medically necessary treatment" means healthcare services that are 12 consistent with generally accepted principles of professional medical practice. 13 Medically necessary treatment includes but is not limited to cognitive 14 rehabilitation therapy; cognitive communication therapy; neurocognitive 15 therapy and rehabilitation; neurobehavioral; neurophysiological; and 16 neuropsychological testing; neurofeedback therapy; functional rehabilitation 17 therapy; community reintegration services; post-acute residential treatment 18 services; inpatient services; outpatient and day treatment services; and home- 19 and community-based treatment. 20 (10) "Neurobehavioral therapy" means a set of medical and therapeutic 21 assessments and treatments focused on behavioral impairments associated with 22 brain disease or injury and the amelioration of these impairments through the 23 development of prosocial behavior. 24 (11) "Neurocognitive therapy" means treatment of disorders in which 25 the primary clinical deficit is in cognitive function which has not been present 26 since birth and is a decline from a previously attained level of function. 27 (12) "Neuropsychological testing" means a set of medical and 28 therapeutic assessments and treatments focused on amelioration of cognitive, 29 emotional, psychosocial, and behavioral deficits caused by brain injury. 30 (13) "Post-acute residential treatment" means integrated medical and SB NO. 295 ENROLLED 1 therapeutic services, treatment, education, and skills training provided in a 2 home- and community-based setting and designed to create the maximum 3 opportunity for correct practice of skill in the context of use to develop new 4 neural pathways to enable the person to avoid rehospitalization and long-term 5 care. 6 I. To the extent that any provision of this Section would otherwise 7 require this state to make a payment under 42 U.S.C. 18031(d)(3)(B)(ii), a 8 qualified health plan, as defined by 45 CFR Part 155.20, shall not be required 9 to provide a benefit under this Section that exceeds the essential health benefits 10 specified under 42 U.S.C. 18022(b). 11 Section 2. The provisions of this Act apply to any new policy, contract, program, or 12 health coverage plan issued on and after January 1, 2027. Any policy, contract, or health 13 coverage plan in effect prior to January 1, 2027, shall convert to conform to the provisions 14 of this Act on or before the renewal date, but no later than January 1, 2028. Title: WILDLIFE/FISHERIES DEPT:  Increases the membership of the Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries Commission. (See Act) (EG +$2,752 GF EX See Note)
Summary: WILDLIFE/FISHERIES DEPT:  Increases the membership of the Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries Commission. (See Act) (EG +$2,752 GF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 624 296 SLS 26RS KLEINPETER Author: Subject: Louisiana Department of Wildlife and Fisheries Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries Commission March 3, 2026 4:53 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Richie Anderson Present law provides that the Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries Commission consists of seven members to be appointed by the governor. Proposed law increases the membership from (7) seven to (9) nine members. Proposed law clarifies that these nine members shall be appointed by the governor, six shall be electors from the state at large, and three members shall be electors of the coastal parishes and representatives of the commercial fishing and fur industries. Proposed law provides that six members shall serve overlapping terms of six years and three members shall serve a term concurrent with that of the governor. Proposed law provides that no member that has served for six or more years shall be eligible for reappointment. Contingent upon voter approval of the constitutional amendment proposed in SB 249 of the 2026 RS on November 3, 2026. The proposed law increases SGF expenditures associated with travel reimbursements and per diems for the new commissioners. Adding two members to the Louisiana Wildlife and Fisheries Commission, these members will be eligible to receive travel reimbursement of $0.70 per mile, along with a $25 per diem per meeting. For illustrative purposes, in FY 25, the Louisiana Department of Wildlife and Fisheries (LDWF) reimbursed $9,634 for the seven commission members, or approximately $1,376 per member. This would result in an approximate increase of $2,752 annually. LDWF reports that it will absorb these costs and any additional administrative duties using existing staff and resources. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental revenues as a result of this measure. Title: INSURANCE RATES:  Provides a process for insurers to assert the right to prevent public inspection of certain filing information based on a claim of trade secret. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 297 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides that all rates, supplementary rate information, and any supporting information filed with commissioner of insurance in support of a rate filing shall be open to public inspection, e
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 297 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides that all rates, supplementary rate information, and any supporting information filed with commissioner of insurance in support of a rate filing shall be open to public inspection, except for information that is confidential, trade secret, or proprietary. Further authorizes the commissioner to determine if the information claimed to be confidential, trade secret, or proprietary is in fact confidential, trade secret, or proprietary. Proposed law retains present law. Proposed law requires that the insurer claiming documents or information contain trade secrets file with the commissioner a notice of request for nondisclosure on the basis of trade secret protection. Further provides that failure to file the notice constitutes a waiver of a claim of trade secrets. Proposed law provides criteria to determine if a claim of nondisclosure is correct. Proposed law requires the party submitting a notice of trade secret to the commissioner to include a sworn affidavit that states the basis for the claim of trade secret. Proposed law provides that certain information shall not be considered a trade secret. Proposed law requires the commissioner to employ an independent third-party entity to conduct an independent review of trade secret claims submitted at least once every other year, and to submit a report to the commissioner, president of the Senate, and speaker of the House of Representatives. Proposed law provides penalties for knowingly asserting a false claim of trade secret protection to conceal unlawful financial practices. Effective August 1, 2026. (Amends R.S. 22:1464(D))

[R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 298 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides for items that may and may not be included in an insurer's rates. Proposed law retains present law. Proposed law prohibits an insurer from including advertising expenses in determining its rates. Present law defines "institutional advertising expenses". Proposed law repeals present law. (Amends R.S. 22:1452(C)(7) and 1454(B)(3); repeals R.S. 22:1452(C)(9.1))

Title: INSURANCE DEPARTMENT:  Allows nonprofit organizations to provide additional resources to cover the cost of installing a fortified roof. (gov sig)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 299 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law establishes the Louisiana Fortify Homes Program and authorizes the commissioner of insurance to provide financial grants for insurable properties that have a homestead exemption to retrofit
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 299 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law establishes the Louisiana Fortify Homes Program and authorizes the commissioner of insurance to provide financial grants for insurable properties that have a homestead exemption to retrofit a roof that meets or exceeds the fortified roof standard of the Insurance Institute for Business and Home Safety. Proposed law retains present law. Proposed law authorizes a nonprofit organization to provide additional support and resources to a recipient of a grant to assist in covering the cost of installing a fortified roof. Effective upon signature of the governor or lapse of time for gubernatorial action. (Adds R.S. 22:1483.1(G))

Title: PUBLIC LANDS:  Provides relative to surplus immovable property for affordable housing. (7/1/26)

[R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 301 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides for the surplus of immovable property deemed nonessential by state agencies. Further requires the division of administration to prepare land use recommendations for the property. Pr
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 301 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides for the surplus of immovable property deemed nonessential by state agencies. Further requires the division of administration to prepare land use recommendations for the property. Present law provides for the transfer of surplus property. Proposed law retains present law and establishes a process for transferring surplus immovable property to housing entities for the construction of affordable housing. Proposed law defines "affordable housing", "affordable housing entity", "deeply affordable housing", "very low-income resident", "low-income resident", "moderate-income resident", "permanent supportive housing", and "surplus immovable property". Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to make an inventory of surplus immovable property on its website. Provides that the governor may determine that a property is essential to the state and requires that the governor provide written reasons for the determination. Proposed law provides that an award of property shall be made at least once a year. Proposed law provides that an affordable housing entity may submit a proposal to build affordable housing on a parcel and that proposals be evaluated by the board of the La. Housing Corp. based on inclusion of supportive housing, proportion of deeply affordable units, type of ownership, on-site disability services, and acceptance of tenant-based vouchers. Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to notify the commissioner of administration of the number of properties transferred, number of housing units planned, and the type of developments its approves. Proposed law provides for the commissioner to oversee the cooperative endeavor agreement (CEAs) between the La. Housing Corp. and the affordable housing entity. Further allows the entity to exchange development services in exchange for property rights. Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to provide a report to the legislature, governor, and commissioner providing an inventory of parcels transferred, total number of parcels conveyed, inventory of developments approved, and the total number of housing units planned and completed. Present law requires the division to provide a land management best use recommendation report to the legislative committees on natural resources and environment and any legislator in whose district a surplus immovable property is located. Prohibits the division from implementing its recommendation, unless approved by the legislative committees on natural resources and environment. Proposed law changes the report recipient to the La. Housing Corp and removes the requirement for legislative committee approval prior to division implementation of best use recommendations. Further requires the La. Housing Corp. to decide whether to retain the property for disposition pursuant to proposed law within 90 days of receipt of the division report. If the parcel is not retained for affordable housing development, proposed law requires the division to implement its recommendations. Present law requires the division submit an annual report to the legislative committees on natural resources and environment, providing the amount of property sold, agencies that held the property, costs, and proceeds from sales, and an updated inventory of property still owned by the state. Proposed law changes the report recipient to the legislature and the governor. Effective July 1, 2026. (Amends R.S. 41:140(B), (C), and (F); adds R.S. 40:600.112)

Title: COURTS:  Provides for the election sections in the First Judicial District Court and for one judge to be elected at large. (gov sig) (OR NO IMPACT LF EX See Note)
Summary: COURTS:  Provides for the election sections in the First Judicial District Court and for one judge to be elected at large. (gov sig) (OR NO IMPACT LF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note OFFICE OF LEGISLATIVE AUDITOR Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 267 302 SLS 26RS JENKINS Author: Subject: First Judicial District Court Election of Judges to the First Judicial District Court March 11, 2026 4:48 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Elle Craft Purpose of Bill: This bill provides for the restructuring of judicial elections within the First Judicial District Court, which solely encompasses Caddo Parish, by reallocating one judgeship seat (Division F) from Election Section Three to a district- wide at-large seat, resulting in four judges assigned to Election Section One, three judges to Election Section Two, three judges to Election Section Three, and one judge elected at large. This reallocation will take effect at the next regularly scheduled election following the vacancy of the Division F judgeship due to the currently serving judge’s death, resignation, retirement, incapacity, or decision not to seek re-election. The total number of judges for the First Judicial District Court remains unchanged at eleven. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental expenditures as a result of this measure. An official with the First Judicial District Court (Court) indicated that this bill is not expected to impact the Court's expenditures, as both the number of judges and court operations are expected to remain unchanged. An official with the Louisiana Secretary of State indicated that any costs associated with ballot changes for the new at-large seat are expected to be minimal. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental revenues as a result of this measure.
