Title: PROCUREMENT CODE:  Creates the "Interstate Technology Collaboration Act" to provide for use of cooperative purchasing for information technology system procurement. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX See Note)
Summary: PROCUREMENT CODE:  Creates the "Interstate Technology Collaboration Act" to provide for use of cooperative purchasing for information technology system procurement. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 303 BY SENATOR CLOUD 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 39:1702(A)(1) and to enact R.S. 39:1702(D), relative to the 3 Louisiana Procurement Code; to provide for cooperative purchasing for information 4 technology systems; to provide for an effective date; and to provide for related 5 matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. R.S. 39:1702(A)(1) is hereby amended and reenacted and R.S. 39:1702(D) 8 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 9 §1702. Cooperative purchasing authorized; participation in federal General Services 10 Administration vendor list 11 A.(1) Any public procurement unit may either participate in, sponsor, 12 conduct, or administer a cooperative purchasing agreement for the acquisition of any 13 supplies, services, personal, professional, consulting, and social services, major 14 repairs, or construction with one or more public procurement units or external 15 procurement activities or one or more private procurement units in accordance with 16 an agreement entered into between the participants. Such cooperative purchasing 17 may include but is not limited to joint or multi-party contracts between public 18 procurement units and open-ended state public procurement unit contracts which are ACT No. 485 SB NO. 303 ENROLLED 1 made available to local public procurement units. Procurement units within the 2 executive branch of state government which are subject to the authority of the 3 Office of Technology Services pursuant to R.S. 39:15.1 may also utilize this Part 4 for the procurement of information technology systems and information 5 technology services as provided in Part V-A of Chapter 1 of Subtitle I of this 6 Title. Nothing in this Part shall be construed to expand or limit the authority 7 of the Office of Technology Services as established in R.S. 39:15.1 through 15.3. 8 Such cooperative purchasing may include but is not limited to: 9 (a) Joint or multi-party contracts between public procurement units. 10 (b) Open-ended state public procurement unit contracts which are made 11 available to public procurement units. 12 (c) Contracts between a public procurement unit and an external 13 procurement activity under which the public procurement unit accesses and 14 utilizes an existing information technology system belonging to the external 15 procurement activity in exchange for payment of a share of the costs associated 16 with the system. The public procurement unit shall not be required to 17 participate in a competitive procurement process for such contracts, provided 18 that the external procurement activity originally acquired the system by means 19 of a contract procured through a competitive procurement process. 20 * * * 21 D. Procurement units within the executive branch of government which 22 are subject to the authority of the Office of Technology Services pursuant to 23 R.S. 39:15.1 may procure information technology systems and services as 24 provided in Part V-A of Chapter 1 of Subtitle I of this Title from the General 25 Services Administration supply schedules. Such purchases need not comply 26 with the competitive bidding requirements of this Chapter. 27 Section 2. This Act shall be known and may be cited as the "Interstate Technology 28 Collaboration Act". 29 Section 3. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 30 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature SB NO. 303 ENROLLED 1 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 2 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 3 effective on the day following such approval. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Provides for accreditation for public colleges and universities. (gov sig) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Provides for accreditation for public colleges and universities. (gov sig) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 304 BY SENATOR EDMONDS 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 17:3125.1 and Chapter 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 3 1950, to be comprised of R.S. 17:3171 through 3176, relative to public post 4 secondary institutions and governing boards; to authorize the Board of Regents to set 5 policy regarding accreditation of public postsecondary institutions; to authorize 6 public postsecondary management boards to select an accreditor under certain 7 circumstances; to provide for coordination between the Board of Regents and public 8 postsecondary management boards; to provide for implementation; to provide for 9 electronic notification by certain institutions of higher education; to provide for 10 powers; to provide for duties; to provide for prohibitions; to provide for definitions; 11 to direct the Louisiana State Law Institute to make technical changes; to provide for 12 an effective date; and to provide for related matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. R.S. 17:3125.1 and Chapter 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised 15 Statutes of 1950, comprised of R.S. 17:3171 through 3176, are hereby enacted to read as 16 follows: 17 §3125.1. Accreditation; powers of the Board of Regents 18 The Board of Regents shall have the power to set policy regarding the 19 accreditation of all public postsecondary institutions, as provided in Chapter 20 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950. 21 * * * 22 CHAPTER 25-B. ACCREDITATION OF 23 PUBLIC POST SECONDARY INSTITUTIONS 24 §3171. Application 25 Except as otherwise specifically provided by law, this Chapter shall 26 govern the accreditation of all public postsecondary institutions. 27 §3172. Definitions ACT No. 486 SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 As used in this Chapter, the following terms have the following meanings 2 unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: 3 (1) "Board of Regents" means the coordinating body for public 4 postsecondary education that oversees policies, budgets, academic program 5 approval, and facility planning for all Louisiana universities, community 6 colleges, and technical schools, pursuant to Article VIII, Section 5 of the 7 Constitution of Louisiana and R.S. 17:3121. 8 (2) "Management board" means a governing board of a public 9 postsecondary education institution that is responsible for supervising the 10 day-to-day operations of such institutions under the overall jurisdictional 11 planning and budgetary authority of the Louisiana Board of Regents. Article 12 VIII of the Constitution of Louisiana provides that the management boards are 13 the Board of Supervisors of Louisiana State University and Agricultural and 14 Mechanical College, the Board of Supervisors for the University of Louisiana 15 System, the Board of Supervisors of Southern University and Agricultural and 16 Mechanical College, and the Board of Supervisors of Community and Technical 17 Colleges. 18 (3) "Public postsecondary institution" or "institution" means a public 19 college or university that is under the jurisdiction of a management board for 20 postsecondary education created by or under authority of the Constitution of 21 Louisiana. 22 §3173. Powers of the Board of Regents; coordination with management boards 23 A. In coordination with each management board of a public 24 postsecondary institution, the Board of Regents shall have the power to set 25 policy regarding each of the following: 26 (1) Accreditation of public postsecondary institutions. 27 (2) Specialized accreditation of any individual program or unit at a 28 public postsecondary institution where such accreditation is required. 29 B. The Board of Regents shall adopt policies to allow a public 30 postsecondary institution, subject to the approval of the institution's SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 management board, to seek a new institutional accreditor from a list of 2 accreditors recognized by the United States Department of Education or to seek 3 a new specialized accreditor from a list of specialized accreditors maintained by 4 the Board of Regents. 5 C. The public postsecondary institution shall seek an accreditor that 25 prioritizes all of the following: 26 (1) Promotes credential success and workforce outcomes. 27 (2) Promotes educational quality and affordability. 28 (3) Promotes financial stability of the public postsecondary institutions. 29 (4) Ensures appropriate accountability through a rigorous annual review 30 of the faculty. SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 D. Title: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Requires the Board of Regents, Louisiana Works, and other state agencies to develop a publicly available statewide dashboard for career alignment that provides information related to educational and career opportunities. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Requires the Board of Regents, Louisiana Works, and other state agencies to develop a publicly available statewide dashboard for career alignment that provides information related to educational and career opportunities. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 305 BY SENATORS EDMONDS, BARROW, BOUDREAUX, CLOUD, HARRIS, HENRY, JENKINS, MORRIS, SEABAUGH, SELDERS AND WOMACK AND REPRESENTATIVES BROUSSARD, CARLSON, CARPENTER, CARVER, CHASSION, FREIBERG, TERRY LANDRY, MARTINEZ, ST. BLANC AND TAYLOR 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 17:3138.2 and 3138.13(C), relative to the Board of Regents; to provide for 3 Louisiana Works to collaborate with the Board of Regents to create a statewide 4 dashboard for career alignment; to provide for information on educational and career 5 opportunities; to provide for duties of the Board of Regents; to provide relative to the 6 collection and analysis of data on work-based learning; and to provide for related 7 matters. 8 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 9 Section 1. R.S. 17:3138.2 and 3138.13(C) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 10 §3138.2. Degree program and career alignment transparency; postsecondary 11 workforce outcomes dashboard 12 A. The Board of Regents, in collaboration with postsecondary education 13 management boards, Louisiana Works and other relevant state agencies, shall 14 develop, maintain, and make publicly available a statewide dashboard or series 15 of dashboards that provide information on the alignment between 16 postsecondary degree programs and workforce outcomes in Louisiana. 17 B. The dashboard or dashboards shall be designed to assist in the 18 following: 19 (1) Prospective and current students and their families in understanding 20 career outcomes associated with degree programs. 21 (2) Policymakers and state agencies in evaluating postsecondary 22 program alignment with workforce needs. 23 (3) Postsecondary institutions in strengthening program design and labor 24 market relevance. 25 C. To the extent permitted by state and federal law, the dashboard or ACT No. 99 SB NO. 305 ENROLLED 1 dashboards may include but shall not be limited to the following information, 2 disaggregated as appropriate: 3 (1) Degree program completion data. 4 (2) Employment outcomes following completion, including industry and 5 occupational placement. 6 (3) Median and range of wages at defined intervals following completion. 7 (4) Alignment of degree programs with high-demand or priority 8 occupations as identified by the state. 9 (5) Additional indicators determined by the Board of Regents to be 10 relevant to career alignment and economic development. 11 D. The Board of Regents shall ensure that the dashboard or dashboards 12 are updated on a regular basis, use consistent and clearly defined metrics, and 13 present information in a manner that is accessible and understandable to the 14 general public. 15 E. Nothing in this Section shall be construed to: 16 (1) Require the disclosure of personally identifiable information. 17 (2) Create performance funding requirements or mandates for 18 postsecondary institutions. 19 (3) Limit institutional autonomy over curriculum or academic 20 programming. 21 F. The Board of Regents may adopt rules and enter into data-sharing 22 agreements as necessary to implement this Section, subject to applicable state 23 and federal privacy laws. 24 * * * 25 §3138.13. Return on investment analysis; industry-based credentials 26 * * * 27 C. The Board of Regents and Louisiana Works shall develop procedures 28 to collect and analyze data on work-based learning through experiences such as 29 internships, cooperative education programs, apprenticeships, or other similar 30 forms of work-based learning. The data collected on work-based learning SB NO. 305 ENROLLED 1 experiences may include whether the experience is paid or unpaid, the duration 2 of the placement, and whether the placement is part of a registered or 3 credential-aligned program. Any procedures developed by the Board of Regents 4 and Louisiana Works shall ensure the collection and analysis of data necessary 5 to encourage and capture any work-based learning credits an individual may 6 have earned and the appropriate manner in which such credits may be 7 awarded. Title: RELIGIOUS FREEDOMS/LIBERTIES:  Creates the crime of obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship. (gov sig)
Summary: RELIGIOUS FREEDOMS/LIBERTIES:  Creates the crime of obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship. (gov sig)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 306 BY SENATORS EDMONDS, ALLAIN, BASS, CATHEY, CONNICK, FESI, FOIL, HENRY, HODGES, LAMBERT, MCMATH, MIGUEZ, MIZELL, MORRIS, MYERS, OWEN, PRESSLY, REESE, SEABAUGH, STINE, TALBOT, WHEAT AND WOMACK AND REPRESENTATIVES ADAMS, BOUDREAUX, BOYER, CHENEVERT, COX, HORTON AND WILEY 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 14:38.6 and Civil Code Art. 2315.14, relative to offenses affecting the free 3 exercise of religion; to create the crime of obstruction or interference with the 4 freedom of worship at a church or other place of worship; to provide for elements of 5 the offense; to provide for penalties; to provide for definitions; to provide for liability 6 for damages for offenses related to the obstruction of the freedom of worship in a 7 church or other place of worship; to provide for injunctive relief; to provide for 8 legislative intent; to provide for legislative purposes; to provide for an effective date; 9 and to provide for related matters. 10 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 11 Section 1. R.S. 14:38.6 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 12 §38.6. Obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of 13 worship 14 A. Obstruction of the freedom of worship at a church or other place of 15 worship is committed when a person does any of the following: 16 (1) Uses force, the threat of force, physical obstruction, intentional 17 injury, or attempted intentional injury, to intimidate or interfere with any 18 person lawfully exercising or seeking to exercise the right of the free exercise of 19 religion at a church or other place of worship that is protected by the First 20 Amendment to the Constitution of the United States of America and by Article 21 XII, Section 17 of the Constitution of Louisiana. 22 (2) Intentionally damages or destroys the property of a church or other 23 place of worship. ACT No. 379 SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 (3) Willfully denies an attendee, clergy, or member of the staff of a 2 church or other place of worship any of the following: 3 (a) Lawful freedom of movement on the property of the church or other 4 place of worship. 5 (b) Lawful use of any property or facilities of a church or other place of 6 worship. 7 (c) Lawful ingress and egress to and from the physical facilities of a 8 church or other place of worship. 9 (4) Willfully refuses or fails to leave the private property of the church 10 or other place of worship when requested to do so by the clergy, when the 11 person is committing, threatening to commit, or inciting others to commit any 12 disruptive activity that is intended to disrupt, impair, interfere with or obstruct 13 the worship service in a church or other place of worship. 14 (5) Engages in a disruptive activity at a church or other place of worship. 15 B. For purposes of this Section: 16 (1) "Attendee" means a person who is lawfully present in a church or 17 other place of worship, or is engaging in the lawful ingress and egress thereto. 18 (2) "Church or other place of worship" means any building, structure, 19 or defined outdoor area that is owned, leased, or otherwise lawfully possessed 20 by a religious organization that is used for organized religious worship or other 21 religious activities. 22 (3) "Clergy" means the pastor of the church, a deacon, or other member 23 of the religious organization who has authority over the administration of the 24 church or other place of worship or has been designated by the religious 25 organization with such authority. 26 (4)(a) "Disruptive activity" means intentional conduct occurring on the 27 property of a church or other place of worship during a worship service that 28 materially and substantially interferes with the service, including interruption 29 of the service or obstruction of access to any area used in connection with the 30 service, such as a sanctuary, chapel, nursery, children's ministry area, or other SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 designated worship-related space. 2 (b) "Disruptive activity" does not include behavior otherwise prohibited 3 by Subsection A of this Section. 4 (5) "Religious organization" means an entity that is operated for 5 religious purposes and qualifies for exemption from taxation under Section 6 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 (Public Law 99-514, 26 U.S.C. 7 Section 501). 8 (6) "Worship service" means a regularly scheduled religious meeting or 9 assembly held at a church or other place of worship for the purpose of worship 10 or religious observance. 11 C.(1) Whoever commits obstruction of the freedom of worship in a 12 church or other place of worship shall be fined not more than two thousand five 13 hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not less than fifteen days nor more than six 14 months, or both. 15 (2) For a second or subsequent offense, the offender shall be fined not 16 more than twenty-five thousand dollars, or imprisoned with or without hard 17 labor for not less than one year nor more than eighteen months, or both. 18 (3) In addition to any other penalty imposed pursuant to this Subsection, 19 when the offense results in bodily injury of a lawful attendee, member of the 20 clergy, or staff member, the offender shall be sentenced to not less than two 21 years and not more than ten years at hard labor. 22 D. Any person who knowingly finances, funds, or otherwise provides 23 material support or assistance to another person with the intent to facilitate, 24 promote, or cause a violation of this Section shall be deemed to be a principal 25 pursuant to R.S. 14:24 and shall be subject to the same penalties as the person 26 who directly commits the violation of this Section. 27 Section 2. Civil Code Art. 2315.14 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 28 Art. 2315.14. Liability for damages caused by offenses related to obstruction of 29 freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship 30 A. In addition to general and special damages, a prevailing plaintiff shall SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 be awarded court costs and reasonable attorney fees upon proof that the 2 injuries on which the action is based were caused by conduct prohibited by R.S. 3 14:38.6 resulting in the injury to the person, obstruction of the freedom of 4 worship in a church or other place of worship, or damage to property, 5 regardless of whether the defendant was arrested, charged, or prosecuted for 6 such conduct. 7 B. Nothing in this Article shall be construed to limit any other cause of 8 action or remedy provided by law. 9 C. As used in this Article, a defendant includes any person who 10 knowingly aided, abetted, financed, funded, solicited, conspired, or otherwise 11 provided material support or assistance for conduct prohibited by R.S. 14:38.6. 12 D.(1) Recoverable damages pursuant to this Article may include but are 13 not limited to damages for physical injury, mental anguish, emotional distress, 14 property damage, lost income, interference with the free exercise of religion, 15 and any other damages authorized by law. 16 (2) In addition to any other remedy provided by law, a court may grant 17 temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, permanent injunctions, 18 or declaratory relief, as well as court costs and reasonable attorney fees, to 19 prevent the conduct, or to prevent the continuation of the conduct, that is 20 prohibited by R.S. 14:38.6. 21 Section 3. The legislature finds and declares that: 22 (1) The free exercise of religion and the right of individuals to assemble for worship 23 services is protected by the First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States of 24 America and Article I, Section 8 of the Constitution of Louisiana. 25 (2) In 2023, the legislature and the people of Louisiana adopted Article XII, Section 26 17 of the Constitution of Louisiana which provides that "(t)he freedom to worship in a 27 church or other place of worship is a fundamental right that is worthy of the highest order 28 of protection". 29 (3) The intentional interference with a scheduled worship service undermines these 30 fundamental rights to the free exercise of religion and the freedom to worship in a church SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 or other place of worship by disrupting the orderly practice of religion and can create a 2 danger to persons attending the worship service. 3 (4) The right to engage in a peaceful protest is essential to a free society of people, 4 and that right extends to public places that are traditionally open to political speech and 5 debate, including public streets, sidewalks, and parks with minimal constitutional 6 restrictions; however, that right has never been interpreted to authorize the trespass or 7 disruptive activity on private property, such as a church or other place of worship or at a 8 home. 9 (5) Prevention of the willful disruption of worship services and religious activities 10 is a matter of statewide concern. 11 (6) It is a compelling interest of this state to protect the ability of its people to meet 12 and assemble for the exercise of religious worship and other religious activities guaranteed 13 by the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States 14 of America as well as Article I, Section 8 and Article XII, Section 17 of the Constitution of 15 Louisiana. 16 (7) It is imperative to a free society and the survival of a constitutional republic that 17 the state of Louisiana protects attendees in churches or other places of worship so that the 18 attendees are free from intentional acts that materially interfere or disrupt a scheduled 19 worship service or other religious activity that is conducted on the private property that is 20 set aside by the religious organization for constitutionally protected religious assemblies. 21 Section 4. The legislature declares that the purpose of this Act is to enact protections 22 that may be enforced at the state level, by state law enforcement authorities, in a similar 23 manner that federal law enforcement authorities, pursuant to 18 U.S.C. 248(a)(2), are able 24 to protect the free exercise of religion at a place of religious worship by prohibiting the use 25 of force, threats of force, or physical obstruction to injure, intimidate, or interfere with any 26 person exercising their right to worship at a church or other place of worship. 27 Section 5. If any provision or item of this Act, or the application thereof, is held 28 invalid, such invalidity shall not affect other provisions, items, or applications of the Act 29 which can be given effect without the invalid provision, item, or application and to this end 30 the provisions of this Act are hereby declared severable. SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 Section 6. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 2 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 3 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 4 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 5 effective on the day following such approval. Title: PUBLIC BUILDINGS/GROUNDS:  Authorizes the educational display of the Ten Commandments as a historical document and monument. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: PUBLIC BUILDINGS/GROUNDS:  Authorizes the educational display of the Ten Commandments as a historical document and monument. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 307 BY SENATORS EDMONDS AND HODGES AND REPRESENTATIVES ADAMS, AMEDEE, BACALA, BAMBURG, BAYHAM, BEAULLIEU, BILLINGS, BOUDREAUX, BOYER, BUTLER, WILFORD CARTER, CHENEVERT, COATES, CREWS, DESHOTEL, DEWITT, EGAN, FARNUM, FIRMENT, FONTENOT, HORTON, ILLG, MIKE JOHNSON, MACK, MCCORMICK, MOORE, OWEN, SAWYER, SCHAMERHORN, THOMAS, VENTRELLA AND WYBLE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 25:1281, 1282(A), (B), and (C), and 1283 and to enact R.S. 3 49:150.1(J) and (K), relative to educational documents and monuments; to provide 4 with respect to the educational public displays of certain documents and monuments 5 related to the religious history of the nation and the state's culture, tradition, and 6 codes of law; to provide with respect to certain historical documents and monuments; 7 to authorize the Ten Commandments monument on the Louisiana State Capitol 8 grounds; to provide that the commissioner of administration shall select a proposal 9 and arrange for installation; to provide for design, construction, and installation of 10 the monument without cost to the state; to provide for content and limitations; to 11 authorize delegation of the commissioner's responsibilities; to provide for legislative 12 intent; and to provide for related matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. R.S. 25:1281, 1282(A), (B), and (C), and 1283 are hereby amended and 15 reenacted to read as follows: 16 CHAPTER 32. DISPLAY OF RELIGIOUS DOCUMENTS 17 AS CULTURE, TRADITION, AND THE HISTORY OF LAW 18 §1281. Legislative finding 19 The Legislature of Louisiana finds that: 20 (1) There is a need to educate and inform the public as to the history and 21 background tradition of American law and Louisiana law. 22 (2) America and Louisiana's religious history plays an important role in the 23 background of our American law and Louisiana law. 24 (3) The role of religion in the constitutional history of both the United States ACT No. 487 SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 of America and Louisiana is acknowledged by historians as part of our culture, 2 tradition, and history. 3 (4) The basic Basic knowledge of American legal history is important to the 4 formation of civic virtue in our society and, therefore, recognizing the historical 5 role of the Ten Commandments, the Mayflower Compact, the Declaration of 6 Independence, and the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 accords with the nation's 7 history and reflects the founders' understanding of the necessity of civic 8 morality to a functional self-government. 9 (5) The courts have provided vital direction to the Legislature of Louisiana 10 on how to approach the display of historical documents. 11 (6) In 2005, the Supreme Court of the United States recognized that it is 12 permissible to display the Ten Commandments on government property in Van 13 Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677, 688 (2005). 14 (7) In 2019, the Supreme Court of the United States further recognized 15 that the Ten Commandments "have historical significance as one of the 16 foundations of our legal system. . .", in American Legion v. American Humanist 17 Association, 588 U.S. 29, 53 (2019) and, the court also ruled that the displaying 18 of the Ten Commandments on public property may have "multiple purposes" 19 such as "historical significance as one of the foundations of our legal system" 20 and represents a "common cultural heritage". Id. at 54. 21 (8) The Mayflower Compact of 1620 was America's first written 22 constitution and made a Covenant with Almighty God to "form a civil body 23 politic". This was the first purely American document of self-government and 24 affirmed the link between civil society and God. 25 (9) The Declaration of Independence, adopted by Congress on July 4, 26 1776, provides that "(we) hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are 27 created equal, that they are endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable 28 Rights, that among these are Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness". 29 (10) The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 provided a method of admitting 30 new states to the Union from the territory as the country expanded to the SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Pacific. The Ordinance "extended the fundamental principles of civil and 2 religious liberty" to the territories and stated that "(r)eligion, morality, and 3 knowledge, being necessary to good government and the happiness of mankind, 4 schools and the means of education shall forever be encouraged". 5 (6)(11) The Legislature of Louisiana now endorses the prolongation of a 6 uniform, sound, distinct, and appropriate presentation of the story of the role of 7 religion in the constitutional history of the United States of America and Louisiana, 8 which may be publicly displayed in court houses, and other state and local buildings, 9 at the Louisiana State Capitol, and throughout the state of Louisiana. 10 (12) The text of the Ten Commandments set forth in R.S. 25:1282(C) is 11 identical to the text of the Ten Commandments monument that was upheld by 12 the Supreme Court of the United States in Van Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677, 688 13 (2005). 14 (13) It is the legislature's intent to apply the decision set forth by the 15 Supreme Court of the United States in Van Orden v. Perry, id. to continue the 16 rich tradition and ensure that the public has an opportunity to understand and 17 appreciate the foundational documents of our state and national government. 18 (14) The Supreme Court of the United States acknowledged that the Ten 19 Commandments may be displayed on government property when a private 20 donation is made for the purchase of the historical monument. Pleasant Grove 21 City v. Summum, 555 U.S. 460 (2009). 22 (15) It is the intention of the Legislature of Louisiana that this Section 23 shall not create an unfunded mandate on any public governing authority. 24 Government agencies are encouraged to use documents that are printed, or 25 displays that are produced, and made available to the agency at no cost. 26 §1282. Public displays of religious history impacting the law 27 A. Public displays with acknowledged religious history may include, but shall 28 not be limited to, any of the items in this Section. 29 B. The Mayflower Compact, written and adopted in 1620, the text of which 30 reads as follows: SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Mayflower Compact (1620 A.D.) 2 Agreement Between the Settlers at New Plymouth: 1620 3 IN THE NAME OF GOD, AMEN. We, whose names are underwritten, the 4 Loyal Subjects of our dread Sovereign Lord, King James, by the Grace of God, of 5 Great Britain, France, and Ireland, King, Defender of the Faith. Having undertaken 6 for the Glory of God, and Advancement of the Christian Faith, and the Honour of our 7 King and Country, a voyage to plant the first colony in the northern Parts of Virginia; 8 do by these presents, solemnly and mutually, in the Presence of God and one 9 another, covenant and combine ourselves together into a civil Body Politick, for our 10 better Ordering and Preservation, and Furtherance of the Ends aforesaid: And by 11 Virtue hereof do enact, constitute, and frame, such just and equal Laws, Ordinances, 12 Acts, Constitutions and Offices, from time to time, as shall be thought most meet and 13 convenient for the General good of the Colony; unto which we promise all due 14 submission and obedience. In Witness whereof we have hereunto subscribed our 15 names at Cape Cod the eleventh of November, in the Reign of our Sovereign Lord 16 King James, of England, France, and Ireland, the eighteenth, and of Scotland the 17 fifty-fourth, Anno Domini; 1620. 18 C. The Ten Commandments, as extracted from the Bible. The text shall read 19 as follows: 20 "The Ten Commandments 21 I AM the LORD thy God. 22 Thou shalt have no other gods before me. 23 Thou shalt not make to thyself any graven images. 24 Thou shalt not take the Name of the Lord thy God in vain. 25 Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. 26 Honor thy father and thy mother, that thy days may be long upon the land which 27 the Lord thy God giveth thee. 28 Thou shalt not kill. 29 Thou shalt not commit adultery. 30 Thou shalt not steal. SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbor. 2 Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's house. 3 Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, 4 nor his cattle, nor anything that is thy neighbor's." 5 * * * 6 §1283. Context of public display 7 Public displays set forth in R.S. 25:1282(B), (C), (D), and (E) shall be 8 accompanied by a document entitled "Context for Acknowledging America's 9 Religious History as Culture, Tradition, and the History of Law" which shall read 10 as follows: 11 (1) Some documents stand out as pivotal in the religious history of America 12 and Louisiana's legal system, among which are the Mayflower Compact, The 13 Declaration of Independence as a legal foundation for the United States Constitution, 14 the Ten Commandments as one of the foundations of our legal system, and the 15 Northwest Ordinance, which was a primary document affirming faith and the first 16 congressional act legally prohibiting slavery. It is hoped that their study and relation 17 to each other and the history of our state and nation will foster an appreciation for 18 the role that religion has played in the culture, tradition, and legal history of the 19 law of America and the state of Louisiana and prompt further public study. 20 (2) American law, constitutionalism, and political theory have deep roots in 21 religion. American ideals about liberty, freedom, equality, legal responsibility and 22 codes of law, to mention a few, have roots and underpinnings in religion and biblical 23 literacy. The Ten Commandments, which are found in the Book of Exodus in the Old 24 Testament of the Bible, was were one of the earliest written expressions of law to be 25 incorporated in American legal systems. The Ten Commandments, or the law of 26 nature, also impacted the Declaration of Independence which refers to the "laws of 27 nature and of Nature's God." 28 Section 2. R.S. 49:150.1(J) and (K) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 29 §150.1. State capitol complex; allocation of space; maintenance; law enforcement 30 officer and firefighter memorial; Gold Star Families monument; SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 African-American military service members monument; the Ten 2 Commandments 3 * * * 4 J.(1) The commissioner of administration shall permit and arrange for 5 the placement on the Louisiana State Capitol grounds of a suitable monument 6 displaying the Ten Commandments. The commissioner shall designate the 7 location of the monument, which shall be among other monuments, and shall 8 oversee its installation. The design, construction, placement, and maintenance 9 of the monument shall be free of charge to the state. The commissioner may 10 delegate his responsibilities provided in this Subsection to the office of state 11 buildings. 12 (2) The commissioner shall accept and consider proposals for the 13 monument from private entities and shall select the proposal for the monument 14 that will be installed. In selecting a proposal, the commissioner shall consider 15 the appropriateness of the proposed design, the demonstrated ability of the 16 responsible entity to pay the cost of design, construction, installation, and 17 maintenance of the monument, and any other factor he deems appropriate. The 18 commissioner shall make such selection by December 31, 2026; however, if he 19 has not received an acceptable proposal by that date, he shall select the first 20 acceptable proposal he receives after that date. 21 (3)(a) The overall dimensions of the monument shall not exceed six feet 22 in height, four feet in width, and four feet in depth, including any base on which 23 the monument sits. 24 (b) The monument shall use the same text provided in R.S. 25:1282, 25 which is the same text that was used in Van Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677 (2005), 26 that was ruled constitutional by the Supreme Court of the United States. 27 (c) The monument shall include a plaque that includes the text of the 28 "Context for Acknowledging America's Religious History as Culture, Tradition, 29 and the History of Law" as set forth in R.S. 25:1283. 30 K.(1) An outdoor display or monument authorized pursuant to this SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Section shall be maintained in a manner that preserves the structural integrity, 2 legibility, and respectful appearance of the display or monument. 3 (2) The commissioner shall ensure that each outdoor display or 4 monument is maintained in accordance with this Subsection, including 5 providing for any necessary cleaning, repair, or restoration resulting from 6 weathering, vandalism, or other damage. Responsibility for such maintenance 7 shall be assigned to the private entity selected to provide the display or 8 monument or to another private entity selected by the commissioner. 9 (3) If the entity responsible for maintenance fails to maintain the display 10 or monument in accordance with this Subsection, the commissioner of 11 administration shall provide written notice of the deficiency and a reasonable 12 opportunity to cure the deficiency. If the deficiency is not timely cured and the 13 commissioner is unable to secure another private entity to assume responsibility 14 for maintenance, the commissioner may remove the display or monument. Title: PUBLIC WORKS:  Provides for notice of nonpayment of subcontractors on public works projects. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 308 Original 2026 Regular Session Miller Present law provides the definition of "claimant" as any person to whom money is due pursuant to a contract relationship with the owner or a contractor or subcontractor in connection with the building of a
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 308 Original 2026 Regular Session Miller Present law provides the definition of "claimant" as any person to whom money is due pursuant to a contract relationship with the owner or a contractor or subcontractor in connection with the building of any public work. Proposed law retains present law and adds "noncontractual" relationships to the definition. Proposed law provides noncontractual claimant that intends to preserve the right to assert a claim against the bond furnished or against unexpended contract funds must serve a written "Notice to Contractor and Public Entity". Proposed law further provides the notice be served on the contractor and the public entity within 45 days after the claimant furnishes labor, services, materials, or equipment to the public work, and include the name and address of the claimant, the name of the contractor, the name of the person with whom the claimant has contracted if applicable, a general description of the labor, services, materials, or equipment to be furnished, a statement that the claimant intends to pursue the bond or unexpended contract funds for payment if not timely paid. Proposed law provides a non contractual claimant's failure to timely serve notice required precludes the claimant from asserting any claim or privilege against the contractor, the surety on the bond, or the public entity, except as to work, labor, services, materials, or equipment furnished prior to August 1, 2026. Proposed law provides any claimant who does not have a direct contractual relationship with the contractor and intends to preserve the right to assert a claim for unpaid labor, services, materials, or equipment furnished in a particular calendar month must serve a "Monthly Notice of Nonpayment" for each calendar month in which the claimant furnishes labor, services, materials, or equipment to the public work that payment has not been received, within 30 days after payment is due under the claimant's contract. Proposed law further provides the claimant must serve a "Monthly Notice of Nonpayment" on the contractor, the public entity, and the surety on the bond. Proposed law provides the Monthly Notice of Nonpayment be served within 75 days after the last day of the calendar month in which the unpaid labor, services, materials, or equipment were furnished. Proposed law further provides the Monthly Notice of Nonpayment include the project and public entity, the name of the contractor, the name of the person the claimant has contracted, the calendar month or months the notice corresponds with, the total amount claimed as unpaid for the months, that the claimant has not been paid the amount, that failure to pay may result in a claim against the bond and unexpended contract funds. Proposed law provides a claimant who does not timely serve a Monthly Notice of Nonpayment is not entitled to assert any claim or privilege for the labor, services, materials, or equipment furnished during the calendar month or months of the untimely notice. Proposed law further provides failure to timely serve notice does not affect rights preserved to other calendar months for which timely notice was given. Proposed law provides the method of serving notice may be served by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, addressed to the last known business address of the contractor, public entity, or surety, as applicable; commercial delivery service which provides written confirmation of delivery; electronic mail directed to an address which the contractor, public entity, or surety has expressly designated in the contract, in the bond, or in a written notice filed of record with the public entity for receipt of notices. Proposed law further provides service by certain methods are effective when deposit for mailing, delivery to a commercial carrier, or successful electronic transmission, as the case may be. Proposed law further provides the parties can agree to additional or alternative methods of service. Effective August 1, 2026. (Amends R.S. 38:2242(A); adds R.S. 38:2242 (G), (H), and (I))

Title: DONATIONS:  Provides procedures and methods to execute gifts under the Anatomical Gift Act. (1/1/27)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB Original 2026 Regular Session Pressly Proposed law, relative to anatomical gifts, clarifies that no authorization to make an anatomical gift after death is to be construed as consent to any act undertaken prior to death. Provides that revocation
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB Original 2026 Regular Session Pressly Proposed law, relative to anatomical gifts, clarifies that no authorization to make an anatomical gift after death is to be construed as consent to any act undertaken prior to death. Provides that revocation of an anatomical gift constitutes a refusal and bars subsequent authorization by another person unless the donor re-authorizes the gift. Proposed law lists the methods for executing an anatomical gift and methods by which one can refuse to make an anatomical gift. Provides that an unrevoked refusal bars others from making an anatomical gift on the person's behalf. Proposed law prohibits governmental entities, including driver's license authorities, from soliciting or recording anatomical gift authorization or refusal from minors in the absence of a parent or legal guardian and preserves requirement for express parental or guardian concurrence for any representation made to a minor but permits recordings of representations solely for informational purposes. Proposed law defines a donation after circulatory death and after brain death as when determined by a licensed physician based on ordinary standards of approved medical practice when the person has experienced irreversible cessation of spontaneous respiratory and circulatory funds. Prohibits premortem interventions that have a material likelihood of hastening death or manufacturing irreversibility regardless of consent. Provides that authorization to make an anatomical gift after death does not constitute consent to premortem interventions. Proposed law requires disclosure and express authorization for post-determination transport of a decedent to another facility and for continuation of mechanical support following death and standards for lawful organ and tissue recovery. Proposed law provides a limited judicial review mechanism confined to enforcement of existing law and legally effective authorization, refusal, and consent. Limits judicial review to material violations of law and authorizes expedited proceedings where necessary to prevent irreparable harm. Effective January 1, 2027. (Amends R.S. 17:2351, 2354, 2354.1 and 2354.2; adds R.S.17:2351.1, 2354.10-2354.13)

Title: PUBLIC DEFENDER:  Provides relative to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund. (8/1/26) (OR -$158 GF EX See Note)
Summary: PUBLIC DEFENDER:  Provides relative to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund. (8/1/26) (OR -$158 GF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 341 316 SLS 26RS REESE Author: Subject: Office of the State Public Defender Louisiana Public Defender Fund - Minimum Appropriation April 27, 2026 9:54 AM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: w/ PROP SEN COMM AMD Analyst: Daniel Druilhet Proposed law will result in a decrease of $158 SGF expenditures in FY 27 and an indeterminable impact on SGF expenditures in subsequent fiscal years in the Office of the State Public Defender. Proposed law provides that for FY 27, the minimum amount appropriated to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund shall be determined by multiplying the combined number of district attorney (DA) and assistant district attorney (ADA) warrants in existence at the beginning of FY 26, as described in R.S. 16:51, by the actual ADA warrant value in addition to a supplement of $13,885 per DA and ADA warrant. The following are calculations associated with the $158 decrease in FY 27: FY 27 Minimum Appropriation $47,109,510 FY 26 Public Defender Fund $47,109,668 Change from FY 26 to FY 27 ($ 158) Actual ADA Warrant Value $ 56,850 Combined Number of ADA's and DA's x 666 $37,862,100 Supplement Per DA and ADA Warrant $ 9,247,410 $47,109,510 Actual Assistant District Attorney Warrant Value: The actual assistant district attorney warrant value includes the warrant amount described in R.S. 16:11 ($50,000) for each assistant district attorney (624 total), plus the value of retirement and benefits. Salary $50,000 Retirement (12.25%) $ 6,125 Medicare (1.45%) $ 725 Total Per Warrant $56,850 Supplement of $13,885 per District Attorney (DA) and Assistant District Attorney (ADA) Warrant - $13,885 x 666 = $9,247,410 [CONTINUED ON PAGE TWO] Current law provides for the Louisiana Public Defender Fund in the state treasury, with monies in the fund appropriated, administered, and used solely and exclusively for purposes of the Louisiana Public Defender Act. Proposed law requires the minimum amount appropriated to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund be determined by multiplying the combined number of district attorney and assistant district attorney warrants by the actual assistant district attorney warrant value in addition to a supplement of $13,885 per district attorney and assistant district attorney warrant; provides that the actual assistant district attorney warrant value includes the warrant amount described in R.S. 16:11 for each assistant district attorney plus the value of retirement and benefits; provides that the supplement of $13,885 per district attorney and assistant district attorney warrant to be used to fund the office’s administrative and contractual obligations; and grants the state public defender the authority to allocate the Louisiana Public Defender Fund to provide supplemental assistance as required in current law. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental revenues as a result of this measure. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: w/ PROP SEN COMM AMD Analyst: Daniel Druilhet Subsequent Fiscal Years and Proposed Legislation of 2026 Regular Session Proposed law provides that for FY 28 and each fiscal year thereafter, the minimum amount appropriated to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund shall be determined by multiplying the combined number of district attorney (DA) and assistant district attorney (ADA) warrants, as described in R.S. 16:51, by the actual ADA warrant value in addition to a supplement of $13,885 per DA and ADA warrant. To the extent warrants to the district attorneys change in future fiscal years, a corresponding change in allocations to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund will be realized. Current legislation filed during the 2026 RS (HB 660, HB 719, and SB 454) may increase the annual salaries of assistant district attorneys and district attorneys statewide (from $50,000 to $60,000 annually), increase the overall number of assistant district attorneys statewide, or apportion the number of assistant district attorneys relative to population. If enacted, each of these instruments may impact the overall minimum amount appropriated to the Louisiana Public Defender Fund in FY 28 and subsequent fiscal years. Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 318 BY SENATOR REESE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 47:1517(B)(1) and the introductory paragraph of (2), to enact 3 R.S. 47:1517(G) and 1517.3, and to repeal R.S. 47:1517(B)(3) and (4) and (C) and 4 1517.1(B)(4), relative to the tax exemption budget; to provide for the publication of 5 the tax exemption budget; to provide for the contents of the tax exemption budget; 6 to require parish collectors to report certain information; to provide relative to the 7 categorization of tax exemptions; to repeal assessment requirements; to repeal 8 certain reporting requirements on tax incentives; to provide for the creation and 9 requirements of the business tax benefit report; to provide for an effective date; and 10 to provide for related matters. 11 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 12 Section 1. R.S. 47:1517(B)(1) and the introductory paragraph of (2) are hereby 13 amended and reenacted and R.S. 47:1517(G) and 1517.3 are hereby enacted to read as 14 follows: 15 §1517. Tax exemption budget 16 * * * 17 B.(1) The annual tax exemption budget shall be published on the LaTrac 18 Department of Revenue's website, or any subsequent database that may replace the 19 LaTrac system, and shall include the following: 20 (a) Each tax exemption, and its statutory citation, and its purpose. 21 (b) The revenue loss to the state caused by each tax exemption for the three 22 preceding years, the estimated revenue loss to the state caused by each tax exemption 23 for the current fiscal year, and the estimated revenue loss to the state caused by each 24 tax exemption for the ensuing fiscal year. 25 (c) The estimated cost of administering and implementing each tax exemption 26 for the three preceding fiscal years, the current fiscal year, and the ensuing fiscal 27 year. ACT No. 270 SB NO. 318 ENROLLED 1 (d) The tax exemption budget shall also include the following: 2 (i) The number of businesses which receive each tax exemption, credit, 3 exclusion, refund, preferential tax rate, deferred tax liability, or rebate, hereinafter 4 referred to in this Subsection as the exemption. 5 (ii) The parish or location of each business which receives a tax exemption; 6 provided, that if fewer than ten businesses receive a particular tax exemption, the tax 7 exemption budget may group such tax exemption with another tax exemption which 8 also has fewer than ten businesses receiving it. 9 (iii) The information shall be displayed in a manner that identifies: 10 (aa) The industry group by North American Industry Classification System 11 sector. 12 (bb) The number of taxpayers by industry. 13 (cc) The total tax burden by industry group by individual tax before the 14 exemption. 15 (dd) The total value to each industry group for each exemption. 16 (ee) The total tax value by each industry group by individual tax of the tax 17 collections after the exemption. 18 (e) The items contained in Subparagraph (d) of this Paragraph shall be 19 published to the extent that the information is available to the department, on a 20 schedule to be determined by the secretary of the department, beginning with the 21 incentive expenditures, and fully implemented by the date of publication of the 22 Fiscal Year 2018-2019 tax exemption budget on or before March 1, 2020. The 23 secretary shall ensure that the publication shall not include confidential information. 24 (2) The tax exemptions Each tax exemption in the annual tax exemption 25 budget shall also be organized in an additional opening schedule as follows 26 classified into one of the following categories: 27 * * * 28 G.(1) The single sales and use tax collector for each parish shall prepare 29 and submit a tax exemption budget for all exclusions, exemptions, deductions, 30 credits, rebates, and preferential tax treatments applicable to the local sales and SB NO. 318 ENROLLED 1 use taxes it administers in accordance with requirements in Paragraph (B)(1) 2 of this Section. The tax exemption budget required by this Subsection shall be 3 prepared annually and submitted to the governor and the legislature no later 4 than March first of each year, in the same manner and form as required for the 5 tax exemption budget prepared by the Department of Revenue pursuant to this 6 Section. 7 (2)(a) Each collector shall submit the information required by this 8 Subsection to the Louisiana Uniform Local Sales Tax Board, which shall 9 compile, review, and transmit the information to the governor and the 10 legislature. 11 (b) The Louisiana Uniform Local Sales Tax Board may adopt rules and 12 establish procedures necessary to implement the provisions of this Subsection, 13 including standardized reporting formats and deadlines for submission by local 14 collectors. 15 * * * 16 §1517.3. Business tax benefit report 17 A. No later than the first day of September of each year, the secretary of 18 the Department of Revenue shall prepare and submit to the governor and the 19 legislature a business tax benefit report in the manner set forth in this Section. 20 B. The business tax benefit report shall include a listing of each tax 21 exemption as defined in R.S. 47:1517(E) that provides tax savings or a financial 22 benefit to businesses. The report shall not include tax treatments required by 23 federal law, including those relating to interstate commerce, or provisions that 24 are part of the general tax structure of the tax system rather than enacted to 25 provide a targeted tax preference. 26 C. The information shall be displayed in a manner that identifies all of 27 the following: 28 (1) The industry group by North American Industry Classification 29 System sector. 30 (2) The number of taxpayers by industry. SB NO. 318 ENROLLED 1 (3) The total tax burden by industry group by tax type before the 2 exemption. 3 (4) The total value to each industry group for each exemption. 4 (5) The total tax burden by industry group by tax type after the 5 exemption. 6 D. The items required by this Section shall not include confidential 7 information. 8 Section 2. R.S. 47:1517(B)(3) and (4) and (C) and 1517.1(B)(4) are hereby repealed 9 in their entirety. 10 Section 3. The first business tax benefit report shall be due on September 1, 2026. 11 Section 4. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 12 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 13 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 14 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 15 effective on the day following such approval. Title: LAW ENFORCEMENT:  Provides relative to DNA detection of sexual and violent offenses. (8/1/26) (EN SEE FISC NOTE See Note)
Summary: LAW ENFORCEMENT:  Provides relative to DNA detection of sexual and violent offenses. (8/1/26) (EN SEE FISC NOTE See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 320 BY SENATOR MIZELL AND REPRESENTATIVE MANDIE LANDRY 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 15:605.1, relative to DNA detection of sexual and violent offenders; to 3 provide reporting of outstanding CODIS hit data; to provide for a central repository; 4 to provide for an outstanding CODIS hits tracking system; to provide for reporting; 5 and to provide for related matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. R.S. 15:605.1 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 8 §605.1. Outstanding CODIS hit data; reporting 9 A. The legislature recognizes the importance of improving the collective 10 response of the criminal justice system to criminal investigations, including 11 sexual assault investigations; therefore, measures shall be taken to create 12 accountability in the CODIS hit notification and resolution process. Tracking 13 of outstanding CODIS hits is essential to ensuring that scientific work done in 14 the crime laboratories successfully translates into justice in the state, and that 15 future convictions or exonerations in older cases are not lost or buried under 16 new caseloads. 17 B. As used in this Section, the following terms have the following 18 meanings: 19 (1) "CODIS hit" means a confirmed database match that aids a criminal ACT No. 488 SB NO. 320 ENROLLED 1 investigation and one or more of the investigations involved in the match are 2 unsolved. 3 (2) "CODIS hit notification" means a formal communication sent by a 4 CODIS-participating laboratory to a criminal justice agency when a DNA 5 profile from an unsolved case is confirmed to have matched a DNA profile 6 already in the CODIS database. 7 (3)"CODIS hit resolution" means that an agency has taken steps to 8 determine if the investigative lead provided by the CODIS-participating 9 laboratory is probative to the case at hand. The ultimate disposition of the 10 CODIS hit should be either a communication to the CODIS-participating 11 laboratory that the lead has been exhausted or that the lead has probative value 12 and shall be followed up on by collection and submission of a reference DNA 13 sample to the laboratory from the associated individual, if applicable. 14 (4)"Outstanding CODIS hit" means that a criminal justice agency has 15 officially received a CODIS hit notification and that investigative lead has not 16 been cleared, acted upon, or resolved by that agency within ninety days of 17 receipt. 18 C. The state police shall serve as the central repository for tracking of 19 outstanding CODIS hits in Louisiana. 20 D. The state police shall create and operate a statewide outstanding 21 CODIS hit tracking system. The state police may contract with public or private 22 entities, including but not limited to private software and technology providers, 23 for the creation and maintenance of the system. 24 E. The statewide outstanding CODIS hit tracking system shall 25 accomplish all of the following: 26 (1) Track the status of the CODIS hits sent to criminal justice agencies 27 that have not been resolved within ninety days. 28 (2) Allow criminal justice agencies to update and track the status of 29 outstanding CODIS hits. 30 (3) Use electronic technology in order to allow continuous access. SB NO. 320 ENROLLED 1 (4) Provide for cross-jurisdictional data sharing with law enforcement 2 agencies. 3 F. The state police may use a phased implementation process in order to 4 launch the system and facilitate entry and use of the system for required 5 participants. The state police may phase in initial participation according to the 6 region or volume. 7 G. All law enforcement agencies that receive CODIS hit notifications, 8 including CODIS-participating laboratories, investigating law enforcement 9 agencies, district attorneys' offices, and agencies that aid in the identification of 10 missing persons and human remains, shall provide all required information to 11 the tracking system and fully participate in the system no later than January 1, 12 2027. 13 H.(1) After implementation of the tracking system, the state police shall 14 submit an annual report on the statewide outstanding CODIS hit tracking 15 system to the Senate Committee on Judiciary C, the House Committee on 16 Administration of Criminal Justice, the governor, and the David R. Poynter 17 Legislative Research Library pursuant to R.S. 24:771 and 772 no later than 18 March first of each year. The state police shall make public the current report 19 on its website. The report shall include all of the following: 20 (a) The total number of outstanding CODIS hits in the system statewide 21 and by jurisdiction. 22 (b) The number of outstanding CODIS hits added to the system in the 23 reporting period statewide and by jurisdiction. 24 (c) The total number of outstanding CODIS hits that have been resolved 25 in the system statewide and by jurisdiction. 26 (2) For the purpose of the report required by this Subsection, an 27 outstanding CODIS hit shall be assigned to the jurisdiction associated with both 28 the CODIS-participating laboratory who processed the evidence associated with 29 the unsolved case that received the CODIS hit, as well as the criminal justice 30 agency that submitted the applicable evidence to the CODIS-participating SB NO. 320 ENROLLED 1 laboratory. 2 I. The state police shall adopt rules as necessary to implement the 3 provisions of this Section. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: CRIMINAL PROCEDURE:  Provides relative to electronic bonds. (gov sig)
Summary: CRIMINAL PROCEDURE:  Provides relative to electronic bonds. (gov sig)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 321 BY SENATOR KLEINPETER 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact Code of Criminal Procedure Art. 328(C) and R.S. 22:822(A), relative 3 to electronic bonds; to provide for certain conditions on the use of electronic bonds; 4 to provide for requirements on electronic bonds; to provide for a parish registry of 5 electronic bond agents; to provide for procedures for the issuance of electronic 6 bonds; to provide for types of payment; and to provide for related matters. 7 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 8 Section 1. Code of Criminal Procedure Art. 328(C) is hereby amended and reenacted 9 to read as follows: 10 Art. 328. Bail undertaking 11 * * * 12 C.(1) An electronic bond may shall only be executed in a parish where the 13 sheriff has electronic bond software in place. Agents Only agents may be approved 14 by the sheriff to shall execute an electronic bond. A licensed bail bond producer 15 applying for electronic bond authority may shall make an application to the sheriff 16 and shall be domiciled and maintain a principal place of business in this state and 17 possess a Louisiana bail bond producer license for at least three years preceding the 18 date of application. 19 (2) The approving sheriff authority may require any documents deemed 20 necessary to verify the information contained in the application and may establish 21 additional requirements for approval of electronic bond authority within the 22 parish, including but not limited to geographic limitations, proof of local 23 domicile or principal place of business within the parish, geofencing 24 parameters, or other requirements that the sheriff deems appropriate for the 25 administration of electronic bonds within the parish. 26 (3) Any parish that elects to implement electronic bonds shall allow both 27 in-person and electronic methods of posting bail bonds. ACT No. 489 SB NO. 321 ENROLLED 1 (4)(a) The electronic bond provider shall maintain a registry of agents 2 approved by each participating parish sheriff to execute electronic bonds within 3 that parish. 4 (b) Prior to permitting the execution of any electronic bond, the provider 5 shall verify that the agent seeking to post the bond is listed in the approved 6 agent registry for the parish in which the bond is to be posted. The provider 7 shall deny electronic bond access to any agent who does not appear in the 8 applicable registry. 9 (c) The sheriff shall provide the electronic bond provider with timely 10 notice of any additions to or removals from the approved registry. 11 (5) When an electronic bond is used, all of the following procedures shall 12 be followed: 13 (a) The electronic bond provider shall implement multi-factor 14 authentication for each individual bond posting transaction, which shall include 15 at a minimum both of the following: 16 (i) Facial recognition liveness verification linked to the identity of the 17 approved licensed agent executing the bond. 18 (ii) A secondary authentication factor independent of the agent's account 19 login credentials. 20 (b) The electronic bond provider shall ensure that the authentication 21 required by Subparagraph (a) of this Paragraph is performed personally by the 22 approved agent of record at the time of each individual bond posting. The 23 provider shall not permit the use of shared login credentials, shared 24 authentication devices or factors, or proxy authentication by any person other 25 than the approved agent of record. 26 (c) The approving sheriff may, as a condition of authorizing electronic 27 bond posting within the parish, require the electronic bond provider to 28 incorporate a geographic restriction, including a geofence, limiting electronic 29 bond posting to a defined geographic area within or proximate to the parish. 30 (d) The electronic bond provider shall offer automated clearing house SB NO. 321 ENROLLED 1 (ACH) electronic funds transfer as a method of payment for fees associated with 2 the posting of an electronic bond. 3 Section 2. R.S. 22:822(A) is hereby amended and reenacted to read as follows: 4 §822. Criminal bail bond annual license fee 5 A.(1) On premiums for all commercial surety underwriters who write 6 criminal bail bonds in the state of Louisiana, there shall be a fee equal to two dollars 7 for each one hundred dollars worth of liability underwritten by the commercial 8 surety. Except as authorized under the provisions of R.S. 13:718(I)(2), this shall be 9 the exclusive fee or tax on any criminal bail bond premium, including thereto 10 premium taxes owed. In furtherance of the payment of this premium fee all 11 commercial surety underwriters underwriting criminal bail bonds in the state of 12 Louisiana shall, upon submitting the appearance bond and their power of attorney, 13 simultaneously pay to the sheriff of the parish, a fee of two dollars for each one 14 hundred dollars worth of liability on the bail bond being presented for the release of 15 a person on bail. Failure to pay the fee shall prevent the sheriff from accepting the 16 appearance bond and power of attorney. The sheriff may receive the fee by check, 17 or cash, or automated clearing house (ACH) and shall only accept it only from the 18 surety or the agent of the surety. 19 (2)(a) When an electronic bond is posted pursuant to Code of Criminal 20 Procedure Article 328, the sheriff shall accept payment of the fee by ACH 21 electronic funds transfer, and a licensed bail bond producer may collect from 22 the defendant, principal, or indemnitor any fees associated with the posting of 23 an electronic bond that are authorized by law, including but not limited to 24 platform access fees, transaction fees, and ACH processing fees. 25 (b) The fees shall not exceed the actual cost charged to the producer by 26 the electronic bond provider. 27 (c) In the event a surety or agent of the surety presents payment of the fee by 28 an instrument which is returned for insufficient funds, the agent or the agent of the 29 surety shall be prevented from presenting the appearance bonds with their power of 30 attorney attached until the outstanding fees are paid to the sheriff. SB NO. 321 ENROLLED 1 * * * 2 Section 3. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 3 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 4 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 5 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 6 effective on the day following such approval. Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 322 BY SENATORS DUPLESSIS, BARROW, BARTHELEMY, BOUDREAUX, CARTER, FESI, HARRIS, JENKINS, KLEINPETER, MYERS, PRESSLY, PRICE, SELDERS, STINE AND TALBOT AND REPRESENTATIVES BAMBURG, CARVER, CHASSION, CREWS, GLORIOSO, HORTON, LARVADAIN, MARTINEZ, MELERINE, MENA, PHELPS AND WILDER 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 23:386, relative to apprentices; to provide eligibility 3 requirements for the Louisiana Youth Apprentice program; and to provide for related 4 matters. 5 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 6 Section 1. R.S. 23:386 is hereby amended and reenacted to read as follows: 7 §386. Apprentice defined; Louisiana Youth Apprentice 8 A. The term "apprentice" as used in this Chapter is defined as a worker at 9 least sixteen years of age, except where a higher minimum age standard is otherwise 10 fixed by law, who is employed to learn an apprenticeable occupation pursuant to the 11 standards of apprenticeship as provided for in LAC 40:IX.317 in order to fulfill the 12 requirements of LAC 40:IX.301 and who has entered into a written apprentice 13 agreement with an employer, an association of employers, or an organization of 14 employees, providing for a time-based program model with not less than two 15 thousand hours of reasonably continuous employment, a competency-based program 16 model, or a hybrid program, and for participation in an approved program of training 17 through employment and through education in related and supplemental subjects. 18 B. A Louisiana Youth Apprentice is an apprentice, as provided in 19 Subsection A of this Section, who meets all of the following criteria: 20 (1) Is no older than twenty-four years of age. 21 (2) Has entered into a written registered apprenticeship agreement that 22 both: 23 (a) Provides for not less than three hundred seventy-five hours of 24 reasonably continuous employment. ACT No. 490 SB NO. 322 ENROLLED 1 (b) Provides for participation in an approved program of training 2 through paid employment and through education in related and supplemental 3 subjects that results in a high school diploma or equivalent and an advanced 4 industry-based credential, certificate of technical studies, or an equivalent or 5 more advanced credential. Title: HEALTH/ACC INSURANCE:  Provides relative to penalties for violations of the Louisiana Insurance Code. (gov sig)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 323 Original 2026 Regular Session Bass Present law (R.S. 22:18) provides for the commissioner of insurance's general authority to refuse to renew, suspend, or revoke a certificate of authority or license, or in lieu of suspension or revocation of
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 323 Original 2026 Regular Session Bass Present law (R.S. 22:18) provides for the commissioner of insurance's general authority to refuse to renew, suspend, or revoke a certificate of authority or license, or in lieu of suspension or revocation of a certificate or license, to levy a fine not to exceed $1,000 for each violation up to $100,000 aggregate for all violations in a calendar year. Proposed law retains present law but adds an exception that, in the case of a violation pertaining to pharmacy benefits, the commissioner shall levy a fine of $25,000 for each violation per insurer, person, or entity with no aggregate dollar maximum. Proposed law further requires, if a violation is not corrected within 30 days after notice of the fine is received, the commissioner shall suspend or revoke the certificate of authority or license of the insurer, person, or entity. Present law (R.S. 22:1860) authorizes, in the case of a violation of present law regarding the payment of pharmacy claims, the commissioner to issue a cease and desist order and also order one or more of the following: (1) Payment of a monetary penalty of not more than $1,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $100,000. (2) If the health insurance issuer knew or reasonably should have known of the violation, Payment of a monetary penalty not more than $25,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $250,000 in any six-month period. (3) Suspension or revocation of the certificate of authority if the insurer knew or should have known of the violation. Proposed law narrows the monetary penalty option to $25,000 for each and every act or violation, with no aggregate penalty maximum, and repeals the requirement that the insurer knew or should have known of the violation for suspension or revocation of the certificate of authority. Present law provides in the case of a violation of the cease and desist order, the commissioner may order any one or more of the following: (1) Payment of a monetary penalty of not more than $25,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $250,000. (2) Suspension or revocation of the certificate of authority. Proposed law removes the maximum aggregate cap for the monetary penalty. Proposed law requires, if a violation for which the commissioner has issued a cease and desist order is not corrected within 30 days after notice of the cease and desist order is received, the commissioner to suspend or revoke the certificate of authority of the health insurance issuer. Present law (R.S. 22:1969) authorizes the commissioner to issue a cease and desist letter to a person engaged in an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice and to order any one or more of the following: (1) Payment of a monetary penalty of not more than $1,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $100,000. (2) If the person knew or reasonably should have known of the violation, payment of a monetary penalty not more than $25,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $500,000 in any six-month period. (3) Suspension or revocation of the license if the person knew or should have known of the violation. Proposed law, in the case of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice pertaining to pharmacy benefits, narrows the monetary penalty option to $25,000 for each violation with no aggregate penalty maximum. Proposed law further requires, if a violation is not corrected within 30 days after notice of the cease and desist order is received, the commissioner shall suspend or revoke the person's license. Present law (R.S. 22:1970) provides that in the case of a violation of the cease and desist order for an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice, the commissioner may order any one or more of the following: (1) Payment of a monetary penalty of not more than $25,000 for each and every act or violation, not to exceed an aggregate penalty of $250,000. (2) Suspension or revocation of the certificate of authority or license. Proposed law in the case of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice pertaining to pharmacy benefits, narrows the monetary penalty option to $25,000 for each violation with no aggregate penalty maximum. Proposed law requires, if the violation of the cease and desist order is not corrected within 30 days after notice of the violation is received, the commissioner to suspend or revoke the person's certificate of authority or license. Effective upon signature of the governor or lapse of time for gubernatorial action. (Amends R.S. 22:18(A), 1860, 1969, and 1970(A))
