Title: WORKERS' COMPENSATION:  Provides for Occupational Accident Insurance for independent contractors. (gov sig)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 358 Original 2026 Regular Session Abraham Present law defines "independent contractor". Proposed law retains present law and defines "independent contractor occupational accident coverage" as a private insurance that pays benefits to an independe
Content: Section 2. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become effective on the day following such approval. The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 358 Original 2026 Regular Session Abraham Present law defines "independent contractor". Proposed law retains present law and defines "independent contractor occupational accident coverage" as a private insurance that pays benefits to an independent contractor who becomes injured, disabled, or dies due to a work-related accident. Proposed law allows an independent contractor to obtain independent contractor occupational accident coverage that is approved by the commissioner of insurance. Proposed law provides that independent contractor occupational accident coverage satisfies coverage requirements for independent contractors even if the principal is determined to be a statutory employer. Proposed law provides that independent contractor occupational accident coverage provides: (1) Medical benefits of not less than $1,000,000 per occurrence for medical treatment resulting from a work-related injury. (2) Temporary disability benefits consistent with scheduled injury guidelines if the worker is temporarily unable to work due to a work-related injury. (3) Accidental death and dismemberment benefits. (4) Certificate of coverage filed with La. Works. Present law provides that when a principal undertakes to execute any work which is a part of his trade, business, or occupation and contracts with any contractor for the execution by or under the contractor of the whole or any part of the work undertaken by the principal, the principal, as a statutory employer, will be granted the exclusive remedy protections of present law relative to exclusiveness of rights and will be liable to pay to any employee employed in the execution of the work or to his dependent, any compensation under present law which he would have been liable to pay if the employee had been immediately employed by him. Present law further provides that a statutory employer relationship exists whenever the services or work provided by the immediate employer is contemplated by or included in a contract between the principal and any person or entity other than the employee's immediate employer. Proposed law retains present law and provides that a principal will not be deemed a statutory employer of a contractor or his employee when the following conditions are satisfied: (1) The contractor maintains a valid employer identification number or a registered business entity. (2) The contractor executes a written independent contractor agreement. (3) The contractor maintains active independent contractor occupational accident coverage meeting minimum statutory standards. (4) The contractor retains control over the means and methods of performing the contracted work. Proposed law allows contractors in the construction industry, including specialty trades, commercial driver's license owner-operators, and equipment operators, may obtain a independent contractor occupational accident policy. Effective upon signature of the governor or lapse of time for gubernatorial action. (Amends R.S. 23:1021(7) and 1061(B); adds R.S. 23:1061(C) and (D))

Title: WELFARE:  Provides relative to the pathway to independency workforce training and education initiative. (8/1/26) (OR NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: WELFARE:  Provides relative to the pathway to independency workforce training and education initiative. (8/1/26) (OR NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 600 360 SLS 26RS JACKSON-ANDREWS Author: Subject: Louisiana Works Changes timeframe of expansion for workforce training March 10, 2026 6:13 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Darynn Hoppe Present law establishes the pathway to independency workforce training and education pilot initiative for public assistance recipients for the purpose of improving employment opportunities and promoting workforce advancement. Present law provides for an initial program service area and allows the initiative to expand beyond the area after an evaluation utilizing performance outcome driven data and information regarding participant experience. Proposed law provides that the program may be expanded upon the conclusion of the evaluation. Effective August 1, 2026. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental expenditures as a result of this measure. The bill appears to serve as a technical amendment to Act 336 of 2024, which renamed the Workforce training and education initiative, and added additional members to the advisory board. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental revenues as a result of this measure. Title: CIVIL PROCEDURE:  Provides for limitations relative to claims for general damages. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 361 Original 2026 Regular Session Mizell Proposed law provides that except as otherwise provided in proposed law, general damages shall not be awarded in a delictual action in an amount in excess of $500,000, regardless of the number of parties a
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 361 Original 2026 Regular Session Mizell Proposed law provides that except as otherwise provided in proposed law, general damages shall not be awarded in a delictual action in an amount in excess of $500,000, regardless of the number of parties against whom the action is brought or the number of actions brought. Proposed law provides that if the trier of fact finds that a claimant has suffered a permanent mental injury that severely impairs the claimant's ability to be employed or enjoy a reasonable standard of living, general damages shall not be awarded in a delictual action in an amount in excess of $1 million, regardless of the number of parties against whom the action is brought or the number of actions brought. Proposed law provides that the limitations on general damages imposed by proposed law shall not apply if the trier of fact finds that the claimant has suffered permanent and severe physical injury, including a substantial physical abnormality or disfigurement, loss of use of a limb, loss of or substantial impairment to a major body organ or system, or an injury that renders the claimant permanently incapable of independent self-care or the ability to perform life-sustaining activities. Proposed law provides that the limitations on general damages imposed by proposed law shall not apply if the trier of fact finds by clear and convincing evidence that the tortfeasor's actions were intentional or malicious. Effective August 1, 2026. (Adds C.C. Art. 2315.14)

Title: CONSUMERS/PROTECTION:  Provides that surveillance-based price discrimination is an unfair or deceptive trade practice or act. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 362 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provided for unfair trade practices, actions, penalties, and relief. Proposed law retains present law and defines the following terms: "automated decision system", "behaviors", "biometrics",
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 362 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provided for unfair trade practices, actions, penalties, and relief. Proposed law retains present law and defines the following terms: "automated decision system", "behaviors", "biometrics", "consumer", "individualized", "insurer", "personal characteristics", "price", and "surveillance data". Proposed law prohibits any person from engaging in surveillance-based price discrimination and provides that such conduct constitutes an unfair or deceptive trade practice or act. Proposed law provides that a person shall not be considered to have engaged in surveillance-based price discrimination if any of the following occur: (1) Differential prices are justified by differences in the cost of providing a good or service to different consumers. (2) Differential prices reflect discounts offered to all consumers on equal terms in a manner consistent with applicable anti-discrimination laws where all of the following occur: (a) The discount terms are publicly available and accessible. (b) Discount reward membership uniformly applied to any consumer who is a member in a particular group. (c) Surveillance data used solely to offer or administer a discount is not used for profiling, targeted advertising, or individualized price setting. (d) Eligibility for discount is based on information affirmatively and voluntarily provided by the consumer. (e) Loyalty programs do not charge different prices for the same or substantially similar points, credits, or similar nonmonetary system of value. (f) The person operates as an insurer and only inputs risk-relevant data into an automated decision system to determine the amount to be paid for an insurance policy or contract. (3) A refusal to extend credit at specific terms or to enter into a transaction is based on data contained in a consumer report governed by the Fair Credit Report Act. Proposed law provides that a violation of proposed law shall be deceptive and unfair trade practice and subject the violator to any action and penalty provided in present law, including a private right of action. Effective August 1, 2026. (Adds R.S. 51:1430)

Title: VOTERS/VOTING:  Creates the Louisiana Voting Rights Act. (gov sig) (OR INCREASE GF EX See Note)
Summary: VOTERS/VOTING:  Creates the Louisiana Voting Rights Act. (gov sig) (OR INCREASE GF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 633 365 SLS 26RS DUPLESSIS Author: Subject: Inspector General/Attorney General Louisiana Voting Rights Act April 7, 2026 7:10 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Kimberly Fruge Proposed law will result in a significant but indeterminable increase in expenditures for the Office of the Inspector General to establish the Voting Rights Commission and the Office of the Attorney General, to the extent they are required to act as statutory counsel to the Registrar of Voters. Proposed law may result in an increase in SGF expenditures for the Secretary of State or Local Funds expenditures for Registrar of Voters, to the extent actions are filed against them for violation of voter suppression or vote dilution, resulting in the respective entity needing to alter election practices to comply with the proposed law. Office of the Inspector General Proposed law creates the Voting Rights Commission within the Office of the Inspector General (IG) and provides for five commissioners who will be compensated at an hourly rate equivalent to an assistant attorney general. The IG estimates an hourly rate of $350 and assumes commissioners will work 10 hours per week, resulting in a total cost of $910,000 (5 members x $350 per hour x 10 hours per week x 52 weeks). The LFO cannot corroborate the hourly rate proposed by the IG’s office or the number of hours required annually. To the extent that the hourly rate changes or the number of hours required is different than what the IG anticipates, costs may increase or decrease accordingly. Proposed law also requires the IG’s office to certify any organization that applies to be a nominating organization for the committee. The IG’s office indicates that it would require an additional T.O. position at an estimated $120,000 for salary and related benefits to perform tasks related to certification of organizations. The LFO acknowledges that implementation of the proposed law will result in additional workload, though it cannot be confirmed whether existing positions within the IG’s office can, in whole or in part, absorb this increase. To the extent that the increase in workload differs from what is anticipated by the IG, and existing staff are able to manage the increase, or the IG’s office can utilize a part-time position, estimated expenditures may be lower than those provided in this fiscal note. Proposed law authorizes the commission to hire staff and make expenditures as necessary. The exact fiscal impact is indeterminable, but it is anticipated to be significant if the commission decides to hire additional staff to assist in carrying out its responsibilities. CONTINUED PAGE TWO Proposed law creates the Louisiana Voting Rights Act; provides that no political subdivision or state agency may implement, impose, or enforce any election policy or practice that results in, is likely to result in, or is motivated by, in whole or in part, the intent to result in voter suppression; provides that no political subdivision may employ any method of election that has the effect, will likely have the effect, or is motivated in part, by the intent of diluting the vote of protected class members; provides for guidelines and rules for evaluating voter suppression and vote dilution; establishes the Louisiana Voting Rights Commission in the Office of the Louisiana Inspector General; provides for five commissioners who will be compensated at an hourly rate equivalent to an assistant attorney general; provides for a nominating committee comprised of organizations certified by the inspector general; provides for required qualification of commissioners; provides that the commission may hire staff and make expenditures as necessary to carry out its responsibilities; provides for the responsibilities of the commission related to covered policies and covered political subdivisions; provides for enforcement. Proposed law may result in an increase in SGR or Local Funds expenditures to the extent the Attorney General or political subdivision prevails in an action brought pursuant to proposed law and the court finds the action to be frivolous, unreasonable, or without foundation. Revenues may include attorney’s fees, expert witness fees, and all other litigation or pre-litigation fees and costs. The exact fiscal impact is indeterminable, as it is unknown if and when a court will award the state or political subdivision following an action pursuant to proposed law. The Office of the Attorney General (AG) reports that the AG is designated as statutory counsel for the Registrar of Voters, and to the extent that they have to act as their legal counsel, costs will increase significantly. Title: MALPRACTICE:  Provides relative to the Medical Malpractice Act. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 366 Original 2026 Regular Session Harris Present law provides that the maximum amount recoverable for a claim is $500,000 plus interest and cost. Proposed law raises the amount recoverable per claim to $1,000,000 plus interest and cost and exclud
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 366 Original 2026 Regular Session Harris Present law provides that the maximum amount recoverable for a claim is $500,000 plus interest and cost. Proposed law raises the amount recoverable per claim to $1,000,000 plus interest and cost and excludes economic damages. Present law allows a qualified healthcare provider to be liable for up to $100,000 in damages plus interest and costs. Proposed law raises a qualified healthcare provider's potential liability from $100,000 plus interest and costs to $250,000 plus interest and costs. Proposed law requires a court or fact finder to find a specific amount of future medical expenses and related benefits for a claimant. Proposed law allows a court, after determining that a total award exceeds the $1,000,000 cap, to hold excess damages in a trust with the Patient Compensation Fund to be paid in reimbursement to a patient or to his provider for future medical care. Proposed law defines future medical care and benefits. Proposed law changes the present law requirement that all medical malpractice claims against healthcare providers be heard by a medical review panel, and offers a claimant an alternative to initiate a claim through an affidavit by a board certified doctor. The affidavit shall certify that the doctor has reviewed the necessary records and that there was a breach of the standard of care which caused or contributed to the injury or death of a patient. Proposed law provides a prescriptive period for actions initiated by the affidavit process. Effective August 1, 2026. (Amends R.S. 40:1231.2(B)(1) and (2) and (D)(5), 1231.3(A)(1), (2), and (3), and 1231.8(A)(1)(a) and (B)(1)(a)(i); adds R.S. 40:1231.3(B)(3) and (4); repeals R.S. 40:1231.3(G) and (H))

Summary: WATER MANAGEMENT:  Creates the Watershed Restoration and Conservation Fund. (8/1/26) (EN INCREASE SD EX See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 367 BY SENATOR EDMONDS 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 38:3310, relative to the Watershed Restoration and Conservation Fund; to 3 provide for establishment of the fund; to provide for a purpose; to provide for the 4 transfer, deposit, and use of monies in the fund; to provide with respect to the Amite 5 River Basin Drainage and Water Conservation District; and to provide for an 6 effective date; to provide for related matters. 7 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 8 Section 1. R.S. 38:3310 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 9 §3310. Watershed Restoration and Conservation Fund 10 A. There is hereby established in the state treasury a special fund to be 11 known as the "Watershed Restoration and Conservation Fund", hereinafter 12 referred to in this Section as the "fund". 13 B. The purpose of the fund shall be to support the reclamation, 14 restoration, and long-term stewardship of lands and watersheds within the 15 Amite River Basin impacted by sand and gravel mining operations, with 16 priority given to flood-prone areas and impaired watersheds. 17 C. After allocation of money to the Bond Security and Redemption Fund 18 as provided in Article VII, Section 9(B) of the Constitution of Louisiana, the 19 treasurer shall deposit into the fund one hundred percent of the portion of 20 recurring severance tax revenues collected on sand severed from within the 21 geographic boundaries of the district that would have been deposited into the 22 state general fund after allocations of such revenues otherwise required by the 23 Constitution of Louisiana and the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950. Monies 24 designated for the fund and received by the state treasurer from donations, 25 gifts, grants, or other revenue shall be deposited into the fund. 26 D. Monies in the fund shall be invested in the same manner as monies in ACT No. 761 SB NO. 367 ENROLLED 1 the state general fund. Any interest earned on the investment of monies in the 2 fund shall be credited to the fund. Unexpended and unencumbered monies in 3 the fund at the end of the fiscal year shall remain in the fund. 4 E. Monies in the fund shall be appropriated to the Coastal Protection 5 and Restoration Authority for use by the board of commissioners for the Amite 6 River Basin Drainage and Water Conservation District for use as provided in 7 Subsection B of this Section. 8 F. An annual report detailing the receipts, expenditures, and funded 9 activities of the Watershed Restoration and Conservation Fund shall be 10 submitted by the board to the House Committee on Natural Resources and 11 Environment and the Senate Committee on Natural Resources by March first 12 of each calendar year. 13 Section 2. This Act shall become effective on July 1, 2026. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: PHARMACEUTICALS:  Provides for prescription drug pricing. (8/1/26) (OR +$270,360 SG EX See Note)
Summary: PHARMACEUTICALS:  Provides for prescription drug pricing. (8/1/26) (OR +$270,360 SG EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 245 368 SLS 26RS BASS Author: Subject: Louisiana Department of Insurance Prescription drug pricing March 31, 2026 12:48 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Anthony Shamis Proposed law is anticipated to increase SGR expenditures by $270,360 for LDI and by an indeterminable, but minimal, amount from the Pharmacy Benefits Manager Enforcement Fund in the AG’s office in FY 27. These increases are associated with the prescription drug pricing transparency requirements of the proposed law. There is no anticipated increase in expenditures for LDH. Note: Increased SGR expenditures within LDI result in less monies reverted to SGF at the end of the fiscal year. LDI: Information provided by LDI indicates that any additional duties associated with receiving notices, reviewing submissions, publishing information, and participating in the required annual report can be absorbed within existing staff and resources. IT Development and Maintenance costs ($270,360 SGR): information provided by LDI indicates that implementation will require a professional services contract with an applications development contractor for the development of a pharmaceutical submission portal, modifications to the department’s Regulatory Management System (RMS), updates to the public website, and development of an application programming interface (API) for data exchange with LDH. The estimated cost is based on 1,893 hours of programming at a rate of $120 per hour for development, testing, and support activities, for a total implementation cost of $227,160 in FY 27. Ongoing information technology (IT) maintenance costs are estimated at $43,200 annually, based on 30 hours per month at a rate of $120 per hour, beginning in FY 27 and continuing in subsequent fiscal years. Note: The LFO is unable to corroborate the estimated IT development costs provided by LDI. To the extent that hours required through an applications development contractor to provide the required services may be higher or lower, corresponding professional services costs would shift accordingly. EXPENDITURE EXPLANATION CONTINUED ON PAGE TWO Proposed law provides for prescription drug pricing transparency. Proposed law requires a pharmaceutical drug manufacturer to notify the Commissioner of Insurance and Secretary of the Louisiana Department of Health (LDH) no later than 30 days after increases in wholesale acquisition drug prices by a higher percentage than the preceding year for brand name drugs, generic, or biosimilar drugs; and certain new drugs introduced for distribution in the state that have a wholesale acquisition cost greater than the amount to make the drug be considered a specialty drug. Proposed law requires that any prescription drugs that meet these requirements be reported to the commissioner and secretary, including certain information about the drug. No later than 30 days after receipt of a notice, the commissioner and secretary shall jointly request pricing component data. No later than 60 days from the date of receiving the request from the commissioner and secretary, a reporting entity shall provide notification of pricing component data with a signed or written certification of accuracy. Proposed law requires the Louisiana Department of Insurance (LDI) and LDH to make the provided information publicly available on their websites. Failure of any reporting entity to provide the required information shall be considered an unfair method of competition and unfair practice or act. The commissioner or secretary shall refer reporting entities in violation to the Attorney General (AG). Proposed law requires that by January first of each year, the commissioner and secretary submit a joint annual report to the governor, president of the Senate, and speaker of the House of Representative. Effective August 1, 2026. Proposed law is anticipated to have an indeterminable impact on Statutory Dedication revenue within LDI and the Department of Justice. The LFO assumes fines collected through enforcement activities of the AG’s office will be deposited into the Pharmacy Benefits Manager Enforcement Fund in accordance with La R.S. 22:1870.1, and used first to provide for the expenses of the commissioner of insurance and the attorney general. Title: PHARMACEUTICALS:  Provides for prescription drug pricing. (1/1/27) (EG SG EX See Note)
Summary: PHARMACEUTICALS:  Provides for prescription drug pricing. (1/1/27) (EG SG EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 248 369 SLS 26RS BASS Author: Subject: Louisiana Department of Insurance Provides for prescription drug pricing March 31, 2026 5:17 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Anthony Shamis Proposed law prohibits pharmaceutical manufacturers from offering for use of an Office of Group Benefits (OGB) insurance plan or any other state sponsored plan any drug at a price greater than the price Medicare pays for the same drug. Proposed law provides that an insurer, pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) or person acting on behalf of a PBM shall not provide reimbursement for any name brand drug, biosimilar, or generic drug that is less than the price Medicare pays for the same drug. Proposed law authorizes the the Commissioner of Insurance to enforce provisions of this measure. Effective January 1, 2027. Proposed law is anticipated to have an indeterminable impact on SGR expenditures within OGB beginning in FY 27 and continuing in subsequent fiscal years. The potential impact is associated with drug pricing restrictions for pharmaceutical manufacturers offering drugs through an OGB insurance plan or any other state sponsored plan. The Commissioner of Insurance is responsible for enforcement provisions. LDI reports no expenditure impact, and indicates that any administrative responsibilities associated with oversight and rulemaking can be absorbed within existing staff and resources. Information provided by OGB indicates that requiring pharmaceutical drug manufacturers to offer OGB the same ingredient pricing as offered to Medicare is expected to reduce claims costs. However, the effect on pharmaceutical drug manufacturer rebates passed through to OGB by its PBMs is currently indeterminable. To the extent that the reduction in drug rebates is less than or equal to the decrease in gross claims costs, there would be no impact on OGB self-funded health plan expenditures. Conversely, if drug rebate amounts exceed the decrease in gross claims costs, OGB would experience a net increase in self-funded health plan pharmacy claims expenditures. OGB uses drug rebates to offset self-funded health plan pharmacy claims costs. Proposed law is anticipated to have an indeterminable impact on SGR revenue collections within OGB in FY 27 and continuing in subsequent fiscal years. This legislation prohibits an insurer, PBM, or person acting on behalf of a PBM from reimbursing any name brand drug, biosimilar, or generic drug at an amount less than the price Medicare pays for the same drug. The impact on SGR revenue collections is dependent upon the level of claims expenditure savings resulting from pharmaceutical drug manufacturers offering OGB the same ingredient pricing as Medicare. To the extent such savings are greater than or equal to revenues generated through drug rebates, no additional SGR revenue will be required. Conversely, if revenues generated from drug rebates exceed the cost savings associated with reduced pricing, OGB may need to seek an increase in SGR authority derived primarily from premiums collected from active and retired plan members and their employing agencies. Such increases would be in addition to the anticipated need to increase premium rates in future plan years to address medical and pharmacy cost trends. If OGB’s fund balance is projected to fall below the actuarially recommended minimum fund balance target of $309 million in any ensuing fiscal year, OGB may be required to implement premium rate increases and/or reduce medical and pharmacy claims expenditures by modifying self-funded health plan benefits and/or adjusting existing eligibility requirements. As of January 31, 2026, OGB reports a fund balance of $406 million. REVENUE EXPLANATION EXPENDITURE EXPLANATION Provides for prescription drug pricing. Present law requires the board to do the following: (1) Grant licenses to qualified persons. (2)
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 370 Original 2026 Regular Session Miller Present law provides relative to the powers and duties of the State Licensing Board for Contractors (board). Present law requires the board to do the following: (1) Grant licenses to qualified persons. (2) Hold hearings in accordance with the provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act to consider violations of present law and the rules and regulations of the board. (3) Suspend or revoke any license for any cause as provided in present law and refuse to grant any license for any cause which would be grounds for revocation or suspension of a license. (4) Issue fines, penalties, and other costs for violations of present law. (5) Waive the trade portion of the examination for any applicant holding a similar certification from another state that the applicant passed the examination upon a written certification from the licensing state. (6) Publish and distribute materials containing information as it deems proper to accomplish the purpose of present law. Proposed law retains present law and adds that the board is authorized to review all substandard construction practices within the state and hold hearings for the purpose of suspending and revoking any license for substandard construction practices in violation of present law. Effective August 1, 2026. (Adds R.S. 37:2153(F)(7))

Title: DOMESTIC VIOLENCE:  Creates a public registry for repeat domestic violence offenders. (8/1/26) (EG INCREASE SG EX See Note)
Summary: DOMESTIC VIOLENCE:  Creates a public registry for repeat domestic violence offenders. (8/1/26) (EG INCREASE SG EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 638 371 SLS 26RS ABRAHAM Author: Subject: Department of Public Safety; Office of State Police Repeat domestic violence offenders March 9, 2026 5:45 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: REVISED Analyst: Katherine Granier Proposed law is anticipated to increase SGF expenditures by $610,195 within the Department of Public Safety and Corrections, Public Safety Service (DPS), Office of State Police beginning in FY 27 and subsequent fiscal years reflecting an anticipated increase in personal services associated with market rate adjustments and associated operating costs. Also, proposed law may result in a indeterminable increase within the 64 sheriff’s office statewide. Department of Public Safety and Correction, Public Safety Services, State Police - The proposed law requires the LA Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information within State Police to develop and maintain the statewide registry. DPS reports needing four (4) additional staff within the Office of State Police to perform the duties and responsibilities related to the registry. The anticipated staffing and technology need is based upon State Police experience maintaining the State Sex Offender and Child Predator Registry website. The staffing estimate includes salaries and related benefits for one Criminal Records Supervisor and three (3) Criminal Records Analysts 3 positions including overtime ($236,585). Other recurring costs include office supplies ($3,000), document maintenance ($48,000), professional services contract outside counsel, if challenged on the constitutionally of the law ($150,000), and IAT to Office of Technology Services (OTS) for telephone, computer equipment, storage, development and maintenance ($650,000). In subsequent fiscal years, the fiscal note presumes a 3% increase in salaries for market rate adjustments and related benefits as the LFO presumes positions will be classified. The detailed projected budget can be found on page 2. Continue on Page 2 Proposed law creates the Habitual Domestic Violence Offender Registry. Proposed law requires the LA Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information within the LA State Police to develop and maintain the registry. Proposed law requires the offender within three business days after release to register. Proposed law requires offenders who, after August 1, 2027, received a second or subsequent conviction for certain violations when the victim is a family member, household member, or dating partner to register. Proposed law provides that the court will provide written notice to a habitual domestic violence offender that they are required within three days to register upon release from incarceration or as a condition of probation and parole. Proposed law provides that the court will impose, in addition to any other penalty, a $150 fine that the offender will pay to the clerk of court. Proposed law effective 8/01/2026 for convictions after 8/01/2027. Proposed law may increase SGR associated with the $150 fine to register within three (3) days upon release from incarceration or as a condition of probation or parole. Local revenue may increase due to fines for the following: (1) failure to register and notify as a habitual domestic violence offender as required by proposed law up to $1,000 or imprisonment for up to one (1) year and will have any outstanding probation revoked; and/or (2) failure to provide notice of relocation as required by proposed law shall be fined not more than $500, or imprisoned for not more than six months, or both. Department of Public Safety and Correction, Correction Services (DPS&C-CS) - Proposed law provides failure to register and notify as a habitual domestic violence offender as required by proposed law up to $1,000 or imprisonment for up to one (1) year, with or without hard labor, and will have any outstanding probation revoked. To the extent that offenders sustain a felony-grade conviction for violation of the proposed law, DPS&C-CS will sustain an indeterminable increase in expenditures. For those convicted, sentenced, and then subsequently housed in a state facility, DPS&C-CS will sustain expenditures of $107.60 per offender per day. For those housed in local facilities, DPS&C-CS will sustain expenditures of $29.39 per offender per day. DPS&C-CS advises that impacts on offender populations are anticipated to impact the number of offenders held in local facilities, and that in managing its offender population, it seeks to fill all beds in state facilities first, then assigns overflow offenders to local facilities. To the extent that offenders sustain a misdemeanor conviction for violation of the proposed law, local governing authorities will sustain Local Funds expenditures. The exact fiscal impact of the passage of this legislation to local governing authorities is indeterminable, since it is not known how many people will be convicted and incarcerated in local facilities, nor the length of the sentences assessed with those convictions as a result of its potential enactment. Local Sheriff’s Offices and Municipal Police Departments - The Louisiana Sheriff’s Association reports a $3.4 M increase in salary and equipment costs throughout all 64 sheriff’s offices to implement this measure. The LFO cannot corroborate that the requirements of the proposed law will create a workload level sufficient to require a $3.4 M increase in salary and equipment expenditures. Therefore, salary and equipment expenditures are not reflected in the expenditures box above. To the extent the additional workload is less than what the Sheriffs anticipate, either wholly or partially, using existing staff and resources, the proposed law may be achievable with lower costs. Proposed law provides that a habitual domestic violence offender who is required to register will provide certain information to the sheriff’s department in any parish in which he resides. The expenditure impact of inputting this information into the registry will vary by sheriff’s offices and police departments and depend on the number of convicted persons living in that parish or municipality. Although the proposed law is silent on whether sheriff’s offices and municipal police departments may assess annual registration fees, the Legislative Fiscal Office (LFO) presumes that local law enforcement will assess some amount of fee to defray the costs of maintaining a register similar to the State Sex Offender and Child Predator Registry; however, the LFO has not received confirmation from any sheriff’s office or police department that a registration fee will be assessed. Local Clerk of Courts - Proposed law provides that the court will provide written notice to a habitual domestic violence offender that he is required within three (3) days to register upon release from incarceration or as a condition of his probation or parole. The offender will pay the clerk of court the $150 fine to register, and the clerk of court will remit the fine to the bureau within seven (7) days of receipt to defray the costs associated with registration. The expenditure impact of to the clerk of court offices will vary. Title: SHERIFFS:  Provides relative to state inmates housed in a parish jail or institution and establishes criteria for additional per diem funding. (gov sig)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 373 Original 2026 Regular Session Cloud Present law requires any individual subject to confinement in a state adult penal or correctional institution to be committed to the Department of Public Safety and Corrections (department). Further authori
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 373 Original 2026 Regular Session Cloud Present law requires any individual subject to confinement in a state adult penal or correctional institution to be committed to the Department of Public Safety and Corrections (department). Further authorizes the department to house inmates at a parish jail or institution in certain circumstances and requires the department to pay a daily sum per inmate to each parish sheriff or to the governing authority that operates the jail or institution. Proposed law retains present law. Proposed law requires that any subsequent increase in the daily sum provided for in present law on or after January 1, 2026, is contingent upon compliance with basic jail guidelines approved by the department, all applicable safety and sanitation laws and regulations, and criteria established by the department by rule. Proposed law requires the department to adopt rules and regulations, in consultation with the sheriff or the governing authority that operates the parish jail, for the implementation of proposed law. Proposed law requires the rules to establish criteria for eligibility to receive additional per diem funding, including benchmarks and reporting requirements. Such criteria shall include but not be limited to requirements designed to: (1) Mitigate the state's exposure to liability risks such as addressing critical facility upgrades and repairs. (2) Ensure safe, sanitary, and code-compliant conditions. (3) Provide and upgrade adequate technology infrastructure, if applicable. Proposed law creates the "Transitional Workforce and Vocational Training Pilot Program" within the department for state inmates that are in custody of the sheriff or the governing authority that operates the parish jail. Requires the department to implement the pilot program no later than January 1, 2027. Proposed law requires the pilot program to provide workforce and vocational training to inmates in high-demand skilled trades to address critical workforce gaps and industry shortages while strengthening reentry outcomes. Proposed law requires the secretary of the department to select the location and number of inmates in the pilot program. Proposed law shall be implemented only to the extent that funds are appropriated for this purpose. Proposed law requires the secretary of the department to submit a report on the overall success of the pilot program to the Joint Legislative Committee on the Budget, the Senate Committee on Judiciary B, and the House Committee on Administration of Criminal Justice no later than January 1, 2029. Requires the report to include a recommendation that the program be continued, expanded, or terminated. Proposed law requires the department, in consultation with the sheriff or the governing authority that operates the parish jail, to adopt rules and regulations, in accordance with the APA, to implement provisions of proposed law. Effective upon signature of the governor or lapse of time for gubernatorial action. (Amends R.S. 15:824(B)(1)(a); adds R.S. 15:828.5)

Title: SPECIAL DISTRICTS:  Provides for uniform procedure for the creation of college economic development districts for any college or university in any municipality or parish. (gov sig)
Summary: SPECIAL DISTRICTS:  Provides for uniform procedure for the creation of college economic development districts for any college or university in any municipality or parish. (gov sig)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 374 BY SENATOR CATHEY 1 AN ACT 2 To enact Chapter 51 of Title 33 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950, to be comprised 3 of R.S. 33:9731, relative to statewide college economic development districts; to 4 provide relative to cooperative economic development in and around universities in 5 the state; to provide for the creation of special taxing districts; to provide relative to 6 promulgation of rules and regulations by the district; to provide for the governance 7 and powers and duties of the district, including the authority to levy taxes and special 8 assessments; to authorize the district to incur debt and to pledge tax increments 9 repayment thereof; to provide relative to sales and use tax increment; to provide for 10 terms and definitions; to provide for procedure and requirements; to provide for term 11 of existence of the district; to provide for an effective date; and to provide for related 12 matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. Chapter 51 of Title 33 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950, 15 comprised of R.S. 33:9731, is hereby enacted to read as follows: 16 CHAPTER 51. STATEWIDE COLLEGE ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT 17 DISTRICT IMPLEMENTATION PROCEDURE 18 §9731. College economic development district; statewide; implementation 19 procedure 20 A.(1) It is the intention of the legislature to establish a uniform 21 procedure for the creation of college economic development districts for any 22 college or university in Louisiana, with the consent of the appropriate local 23 governing authority. For purposes of this Section, the "appropriate local 24 governing authority" means the governing authority of the municipality, if the 25 subdivision or subdivisions are located within a municipality, or the governing 26 authority of the parish if the subdivision or subdivisions are not located within 27 a municipality. ACT No. 499 SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 (2) A college economic development district, created pursuant to this 2 Section, shall include property owned by the college or institution creating the 3 district to provide for cooperative economic and community development 4 among the college, the local governmental subdivision, the state, and the owners 5 of property in the district. The terms "college economic development district" 6 or "district" hereafter refer to a district created pursuant to this Section. Any 7 college economic development district is a political subdivision of the state 8 having all of the rights, powers, privileges, and immunities afforded by law and 9 the Constitution of Louisiana to political subdivisions of the state, subject to the 10 limitations provided for in this Section. 11 B. A college economic and development district, created pursuant to this 12 Section, shall be created upon the adoption of an ordinance by the appropriate 13 local governing authority. 14 C.(1) Any district created pursuant to this Section shall be a body politic 15 and corporate and shall be known by the name provided for in the ordinance 16 creating the district, hereinafter referred in this Section as the "district". The 17 district shall be a political subdivision of the state as defined in the Constitution 18 of Louisiana. 19 (2) The boundaries of a district shall be coterminous with the boundaries 20 of one or more subdivisions within a parish as established in an official 21 subdivision plat filed with the clerk of court for the parish and shall not include 22 any residential property. 23 D.(1) A college economic development district shall be administered and 24 governed by a board of commissioners, referred to in this Section as the 25 "board". 26 (2) The board shall be comprised as follows: 27 (a) The highest executive officer of the college shall appoint four persons 28 and shall serve as a member of the board and chairman of the board as long as 29 he is the highest executive officer of the university. 30 (b) Two of the members of the board shall be representatives from SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 businesses within the district. 2 (c) The president of the Senate and the speaker of the House of 3 Representatives shall jointly appoint one member of the legislature to serve on 4 the board. 5 (3) Members shall serve five-year terms after initial terms as provided 6 by the ordinance creating the district. The president shall serve as long as he is 7 president of the university. Two members shall serve an initial term of two 8 years, and two members shall serve an initial term of three years, as determined 9 by lot at the first meeting of the board. Each member of the board shall 10 continue to serve until reappointed or a successor is duly appointed. Any 11 vacancy in the membership of the board shall be filled in the manner of the 12 original appointment for the unexpired term. If an appointment to fill a vacancy 13 is not made within sixty days, the board shall appoint an interim successor to 14 serve until the position is filled by the appointing authority. 15 (4) Any member of the board may be removed by a three-fourths vote 16 of the remaining membership of the board for cause, which may include failure 17 to attend at least one-half of the meetings of the board in a two-year period. 18 (5) The members of the board shall serve without salary or per diem. 19 The board may reimburse any member for reasonable, actual, and necessary 20 expenses incurred in the performance of his duties pursuant to this Section. 21 (6) The board shall elect from its members a president, a vice president, 22 a secretary, and a treasurer, whose duties shall be those common to these 23 offices. At the option of the board, the offices of secretary and treasurer may be 24 held by one person. 25 (7) The board shall meet in regular session at least once per year and 26 shall also meet in special session as often as the president of the board convenes 27 the board or upon the written request of at least three members. A majority of 28 the members of the board shall constitute a quorum for the transaction of 29 business. The board shall keep minutes of all meetings and shall make them 30 available for inspection through the board's secretary or secretary-treasurer, SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 who shall also maintain the minute books and archives of the district. The 2 monies, funds, and accounts of the district shall be in the official custody of the 3 board. 4 (8) The domicile of the board shall be established by the board at a 5 location within the district. The official journal of the district is the official 6 journal of the parish where the domicile of the board is located. 7 E. A college economic development district, acting by and through its 8 board, shall have and exercise all powers of a political subdivision necessary or 9 convenient for the carrying out of its objects and purposes, including but not 10 limited to the following: 11 (1) To sue and to be sued. 12 (2) To adopt, use, and alter at will a corporate seal. 13 (3) To acquire by gift, grant, or purchase all property, including rights- 14 of-way, movable, immovable, or mixed, corporeal or incorporeal, or any interest 15 therein. 16 (4) To enter into contracts for the purchase, acquisition, construction, 17 and improvement of works and facilities necessary in connection with the 18 purposes of the district. 19 (5) In the name of and on behalf of the district, to incur debt and to issue 20 revenue bonds, special assessment bonds, certificates, notes, and other evidences 21 of indebtedness and to levy and cause to be collected certain taxes as provided 22 for in this Section and as may be provided by general law. 23 (6) To regulate the imposition of fees and rentals charged by the district 24 for the facilities and services rendered. 25 (7) To borrow money and pledge all or part of its revenues, leases, rents, 26 or other advantages as security for the loans. 27 (8) To appoint officers, agents, and employees, prescribe duties, and fix 28 compensation. 29 (9) To develop public improvement projects for the benefit of the 30 respective college, either directly with the respective college or through one or SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 more private foundations or nonprofit corporations affiliated with the 2 respective college, or both. 3 (10) To exercise any and all of the powers granted to a college economic 4 development district as an economic development district established pursuant 5 to Part II of Chapter 27 of this Title, including but not limited to the powers of 6 tax increment financing pursuant to R.S. 33:9038.33 and 9038.34 and the power 7 to levy taxes within the district pursuant to R.S. 33:9038.39. The district shall 8 exercise the powers in accordance with the provisions of Part II of Chapter 27 9 of this Title. 10 (11) To exercise any and all of the powers granted to a community 11 development district as a community development district established pursuant 12 to Chapter 27-B of this Title, including but not limited to the power to levy 13 special assessments on property within the district pursuant to R.S. 33:9039.29. 14 The district shall exercise the powers in accordance with the provisions of 15 Chapter 27-B of this Title. 16 F.(1) It is expressly provided that any sales and use tax levied by a college 17 economic development district or any subdistrict created by the district may 18 exceed the limitation set forth by Article VI, Section 29(A) of the Constitution 19 of Louisiana and shall be imposed, collected, and enforced subject to the terms 20 of the resolution imposing the tax and the provisions of Chapter 2 of Subtitle II 21 of Title 47 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950. 22 (2)(a) A college economic development district and any subdistrict 23 created by the district may levy taxes or assessments of any type only after the 24 board has adopted an appropriate resolution giving notice of its intention to 25 levy the taxes or assessments. The resolution shall include a general description 26 of the taxes or assessments to be levied. The district or subdistrict shall give 27 notice of its intention by publication once per week for two weeks in the official 28 journal of the district, the first publication to appear at least fourteen days 29 before the public meeting of the board at which the board shall hear any 30 objections to the proposed taxes or assessments. The notice of intent so SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 published shall state the date, time, and place of the public hearing. 2 (b) The taxes or assessments may be levied only after the board calls a 3 special election submitting the proposition for the levy of the taxes or 4 assessments to the qualified electors of the district or subdistrict, as applicable, 5 and the proposition received the favorable vote of a majority of the electors 6 voting in the election. Any election held pursuant to this Section shall be 7 conducted in accordance with the Louisiana Election Code and held on a date 8 that corresponds with an election date provided by R.S. 18:402(A)(1) or (B)(1). 9 However, if there are no qualified electors in the district or subdistrict, as 10 applicable, as certified by the registrar of voters, no election is required, but the 11 taxes or assessments shall not be levied unless approved by the governing 12 authority of the local governmental subdivision. 13 (c) The powers and rights conferred by this Paragraph shall be in 14 addition to the powers and rights conferred by any other general or special law. 15 This Paragraph shall be construed to provide a complete and additional method 16 for the levy of any taxes or assessments. No election, proceeding, notice, or 17 approval shall be required for the levy of the taxes or assessments except as 18 provided for in this Paragraph. 19 G.(1) A college economic development district may create subdistricts as 20 provided for in this Subsection. The district shall publish notice of its intent to 21 create a subdistrict in the official journal of the district. At least ten days after 22 publication of the notice, the board shall conduct a public hearing on the 23 question of creating the subdistrict. Thereafter, the board may designate one or 24 more areas within the boundaries of the district as subdistricts of the district. 25 Each subdistrict shall constitute a political subdivision of the state and shall be 26 governed by the board. Each subdistrict shall have the same powers as the 27 district and shall be given a suitable name as the board may designate. A 28 reference to the district hereafter in this Section includes any subdistrict created 29 by the district. 30 (2) The boundaries of a district may be changed in accordance with SB NO. 374 ENROLLED 1 provisions of this Section dealing with the establishment of the original 2 boundaries. Summary: SECONDARY EDUCATION:  Provides for the Learn and Earn Act. (gov sig) (EN INCREASE LF EX See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 376 BY SENATORS MIZELL, BOUDREAUX, CARTER, CATHEY, CLOUD, CONNICK, EDMONDS, HODGES, JACKSON-ANDREWS, LAMBERT, MILLER, MORRIS, SELDERS, STINE, WHEAT AND WOMACK AND REPRESENTATIVES BERAULT, BROUSSARD, CARPENTER, CARVER, CHASSION, EGAN, FREIBERG, KNOX, TERRY LANDRY, LARVADAIN, MARCELLE, MARTINEZ, PHELPS, TAYLOR, VENTRELLA, WALTERS AND WYBLE 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 17:81(A)(8), 3996(B)(92), and Chapter 47 of Title 17 of the Louisiana 3 Revised Statutes of 1950, to be comprised of R.S. 17:4201 through 4212, relative to 4 local public school boards; to provide relative to the authorization of career 5 practicums in public high schools; to provide for definitions; to provide relative to 6 cooperative endeavor agreements; to provide requirements for career practicums and 7 cooperative endeavor agreements; to provide relative to student labor, safety, and 8 supervision; to provide for reciprocity; to provide relative to the State Board of 9 Elementary and Secondary Education's rulemaking authority; to provide for trade 10 and commerce limitations; to provide for exemptions; to provide relative to charter 11 school participation; to provide for an effective date; and to provide for related 12 matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. R.S. 17:81(A)(8), 3996(B)(92), and Chapter 47 of Title 17 of the 15 Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950, comprised of R.S. 17:4201 through 4212, are hereby 16 enacted to read as follows: 17 §81. General powers of local public school boards 18 A.(1) Each local public school board shall serve in a policymaking capacity 19 that is in the best interests of all students enrolled in schools under the board's 20 jurisdiction. When establishing board policies, each board shall prioritize student 21 achievement, financial efficiency, and workforce development on a local, regional, 22 and statewide basis. When choosing a local superintendent of schools, each board 23 shall select a leader who shall prioritize student achievement and act in the best 24 interests of all students enrolled in schools under the board's jurisdiction. ACT No. 100 SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 * * * 2 (8) Each local public school board shall have the authority to establish, 3 operate, or enter into agreements for the operation of career practicums 4 pursuant to R.S. 17:4201 et seq. 5 * * * 6 §3996. Charter schools; exemptions; requirements 7 * * * 8 B. Notwithstanding any state law, rule, or regulation to the contrary and 9 except as may be otherwise specifically provided for in an approved charter, a 10 charter school established and operated in accordance with the provisions of this 11 Chapter and its approved charter and the school's officers and employees shall be 12 exempt from all statutory mandates or other statutory requirements that are 13 applicable to public schools and to public school officers and employees except for 14 the following laws otherwise applicable to public schools with the same grades: 15 * * * 16 (92) Career practicums, R.S. 17:4201 et seq. 17 * * * 18 CHAPTER 47. CAREER PRACTICUMS 19 §4201. Short title 20 This Chapter shall be known and may be cited as the "Learn and Earn 21 Act". 22 §4202. Legislative findings and intent 23 A. The legislature finds that Louisiana's long-term workforce 24 competitiveness depends on the development of a coherent, statewide talent 25 development system. 26 B. Compensated, credit-bearing work-based learning aligned with the 27 State Board of Elementary and Secondary Education (BESE) approved career 28 pathways is a critical component of such a system. 29 C. Reliance solely on external employer work sites creates inequitable 30 access to work-based learning for rural students and students in underserved SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 communities. 2 D. Career practicums, as provided for in this Chapter, shall serve an 3 essential educational purpose by advancing established state objectives related 4 to career and technical education, financial literacy, employability skills, and 5 workforce development. 6 E.(1) A career practicum shall operate pursuant to a cooperative 7 endeavor agreement that complies with Article VII, Section 14 of the 8 Constitution of Louisiana and meets the requirements of the Cabela's test as 9 held in Board of Directors of Indus. Development Bd. of City of Gonzales, 10 Louisiana, Inc. v. All Tax Payers, Property Owners, Citizens of City of Gonzales, 11 et al., 938 So.2d 11 (La. 9/6/06). 12 (2) In order to satisfy the Cabela's test, a career practicum must serve a 13 valid public purpose; be an enforceable, nongratutious obligation; and there 14 must be a reasonable expectation of value equal to or greater than the value of 15 public assets provided. 16 (3) Compliance with the Cabela's test is mandatory and shall be 17 demonstrated through the cooperative endeavor agreement. 18 §4203. Definitions 19 As used in this Chapter, the following terms have the following meanings 20 unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: 21 (1) "Business partner" means a private business entity, financial 22 institution, or nonprofit organization that enters into a cooperative endeavor 23 agreement with a city, parish, or other local school board to operate a career 24 practicum. 25 (2) "Career practicum" means a limited scope educational franchise 26 operation conducted on a public high school campus pursuant to a cooperative 27 endeavor agreement, whose primary purpose is to deliver compensated, credit- 28 bearing work-based learning, aligned with BESE-approved career pathways, 29 and related instruction. 30 (3) "Fair market value" means the value of public assets, services, or SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 resources made available to a business partner, including space, utilities, 2 equipment, or other measurable benefits, calculated pursuant to BESE- 3 approved methodology using commercially reasonable valuation principles. 4 (4) "Instructional scope" means the defined operational boundaries of 5 a career practicum that are directly tied to instructional objectives, including 6 pathway aligned activities, financial literacy instruction, employability skill 7 development, and compensated work-based learning. 8 (5) "Student participant" means a student enrolled in a BESE-approved, 9 work-based learning course who participates in a career practicum and is 10 compensated as an employee of the business partner. 11 (6) "Hazardous occupation" shall mean any occupation or activity 12 prohibited for minors under the provisions of the Louisiana Works rules 13 governing the employment of minors. 14 §4204. Authorization; limited-scope model 15 A. A city, parish, or other local school board may establish and host one 16 or more career practicums on Louisiana public high school campuses in 17 accordance with this Chapter. 18 B. A career practicum shall operate exclusively within the instructional 19 scope defined in its cooperative endeavor agreement and shall not engage in 20 general commercial activity unrelated to instructional purposes. 21 C. Career practicums shall align with BESE-approved career pathways 22 and integrate financial literacy and employability skill development. 23 D. Any student participating in a career practicum under this Chapter 24 who performs a credit-bearing internship experience aligned to a BESE- 25 approved career pathway shall be deemed to have satisfied the requirements for 26 an internship for purposes of the state school and district accountability system, 27 as defined by rules of the State Board of Elementary and Secondary Education. 28 E. A business partner shall provide reciprocity under the cooperative 29 endeavor agreement that meets or exceeds the fair market value of public assets 30 utilized and shall fulfill all educational obligations established in the agreement. SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 F. Career practicums may provide customer-facing services only to the 2 extent such services fall within the instructional scope established in the 3 cooperative endeavor agreement. 4 G. Career practicums operating solely within the instructional scope of 5 a compliant cooperative endeavor agreement shall be deemed instructional 6 functions of the city, parish, or other local school board and shall not be 7 classified as commercial tenants. 8 H. Any employee, agent, or representative of a business partner who has 9 direct and unsupervised interaction with students participating in a career 10 practicum shall be subject to the same criminal background check and 11 eligibility requirements applicable to school employees and instructors pursuant 12 to applicable state law and policy of the State Board of Elementary and 13 Secondary Education. 14 I. Where a career practicum includes limited customer-facing services 15 within the instructional scope, the city, parish, or other local school board shall 16 retain full authority to regulate campus access consistent with existing safety 17 and visitor policies. Nothing in this Chapter shall be construed to require 18 general public access to school campuses. 19 §4205. Cooperative endeavor agreements 20 A. Each career practicum shall operate pursuant to a written cooperative 21 endeavor agreement between the city, parish, or other local school board and 22 the business partner. 23 B. The agreement shall include but not be limited to the following: 24 (1) A declaration of the essential educational and public purpose served. 25 (2) Enforceable, nongratuitous obligations of the business partner. 26 (3) An inventory and valuation of public assets utilized, including a fair 27 market value schedule. 28 (4) A reciprocity schedule demonstrating a reasonable expectation that 29 benefits provided by the business partner meet or exceed fair market value. 30 (5) Annual fair market value recertification. SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 (6) A clear statement of instructional scope and operating parameters. 2 (7) Liability insurance naming the city, parish, or other local school 3 board and the state as additional insureds. 4 (8) Indemnification of the city, parish, or other local school board and 5 the state. 6 (9) Safety, supervision, and training requirements. 7 (10) Compliance with applicable federal and state laws governing student 8 data privacy, child labor, workplace safety, and accessibility. 9 C. Each cooperative endeavor agreement shall be public record and 10 posted on the website of the city, parish, or other local school board. 11 D. Nothing in this Chapter shall limit the authority of a city, parish, or 12 other local school board to oversee, monitor, or evaluate the operations of a 13 career practicum. 14 §4206. Student labor, safety, and supervision 15 A. Student participants shall: 16 (1) Earn academic credit pursuant to BESE rules. 17 (2) Receive wages at or above the applicable minimum wage. 18 (3) Be employees of the business partner for wage and workers' 19 compensation purposes. 20 (4) Receive required safety training prior to participation. 21 (5) Be prohibited from engaging in hazardous occupations barred for 22 minors. 23 B. Career practicums shall be structured so that student participants are 24 the primary beneficiaries of the educational experience. 25 C. Career practicums shall comply with all applicable school safety, 26 supervision, and visitor access requirements. 27 §4207. Reciprocity and restricted use of funds 28 A. Business partners shall provide reciprocity as established in the 29 cooperative endeavor agreement, meeting or exceeding fair market value 30 requirements. SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 B. Any funds provided to a city, parish, or other local school board as 2 reciprocity or compensation shall be deposited into a restricted Career 3 Practicum Fund and used solely for work-based learning, instructional 4 equipment, or related educational purposes. 5 §4208. Rulemaking authority; limitations 6 The State Board of Elementary and Secondary Education shall 7 promulgate rules and regulations necessary to implement this Chapter in 8 accordance with the provisions of the Administrative Procedure Act. Such rules 9 shall implement but not waive, narrow, or redefine the statutory requirements 10 of instructional scope, fair market value valuation, reciprocity, or student 11 protections established in this Chapter. 12 §4209. Trade and commerce limitation 13 A career practicum operating solely within the instructional scope of a 14 compliant cooperative endeavor agreement shall not be deemed to be engaged 15 in trade or commerce for purposes of R.S. 51:1401 et seq. 16 §4210. Proprietary school exemption 17 A career practicum authorized under this Chapter shall be deemed an 18 extension of public educational programming and shall not be classified as a 19 proprietary school pursuant to R.S. 17:3140.1 et seq. 20 §4211. Charter school participation 21 A charter school governing authority may authorize a career practicum 22 on its campus in accordance with applicable charter law and the school's 23 charter contract. Where the career practicum involves the use of facilities or 24 property owned by a city, parish, or other local school board or other public 25 entity, any cooperative endeavor agreement required under this Chapter shall 26 be executed in a manner consistent with applicable law governing such 27 property. 28 §4212. Existing programs 29 A. On-campus business operations or work-based learning programs 30 lawfully operating on Louisiana public high school campuses prior to the SB NO. 376 ENROLLED 1 effective date of this Act may continue, provided they serve an educational 2 purpose and comply with Article VII, Section 14 of the Louisiana Constitution. 3 B. Beginning with the 2029-2030 school year, all programs subject to 4 Subsection A of this Section shall be in compliance with all other provisions of 5 this Chapter. 6 Section 2. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 7 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 8 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 9 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 10 effective on the day following such approval. Title: HEALTH/ACC INSURANCE:  Provides relative to pharmacy benefit managers. (gov sig) (OR +$2,265,844 SG EX See Note)
Summary: HEALTH/ACC INSURANCE:  Provides relative to pharmacy benefit managers. (gov sig) (OR +$2,265,844 SG EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 585 377 SLS 26RS HARRIS, JIMMY Author: Subject: Louisiana Department of Health Provides relative to pharmacy benefit managers May 15, 2026 9:46 AM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Anthony Shamis Proposed law provides for compensation, audits, contracts and other requirements pertaining to pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs). Proposed law provides that a PBM may negotiate but not retain rebates and fees, and may only derive income from PBM management fees for PBM management services provided to an insurer or health plan. The PBM management fee shall be set forth in the agreement between the PBM and the insurer or health plan. Proposed law requires that each PBM operating in Louisiana to annually certify to the commissioner of insurance, by December 31, that it is in full compliance with the requirements of the proposed law. Proposed law authorizes the commissioner and any insurer or health plan contracted with a PBM to audit the PBM once per calendar year. The PBM is required to provide information pursuant to an audit within 30 days of receipt of any request from the commissioner, insurer, or health plan. The commissioner may dictate the form in which the PBM will provide information in response to an audit authorized under this measure. Proposed law requires that on or after January 1, 2027, a PBM contract with an insurer or health plan shall: (1) specify all forms of revenue, including PBM fees, to be paid by the insurer or health plan to the PBM, and (2) acknowledge that spread pricing is not permitted in accordance with R.S. 22:1867. Proposed law provides that, in addition to any other civil or criminal penalty authorized by law, a violation of proposed law shall be punishable by the commissioner through a civil monetary penalty not to exceed $1,000 per claim. Proposed law becomes effective upon signature of the governor, or after the lapse of time for gubernatorial action. Proposed law is anticipated to increase SGR expenditures within the Louisiana Department of Insurance (LDI) by $2.3 M in FY 27, with costs increasing in future fiscal years. This increase is associated with the creation of a pharmacy benefit manager (PBM) enforcement section within the LDI’s Office of Health, Life, and Annuity in order to expand the department’s regulatory oversight of PBM activities and ensure adherence to the legislation’s requirements. The LDI reports it will need to add six (6) T.O. positions, including one (1) Pharmacist Chief Compliance Officer T.O. position, one (1) Pharmacist Compliance Officer T.O. position, one (1) Insurance Supervisor T.O. position, two (2) Insurance Specialist 4 T.O. positions, and one (1) Attorney 4 T.O. position, along with associated operating and professional services expenses and one-time equipment costs. The proposed legislation is expected to increase regulatory oversight of PBMs within LDI, including annual certification fillings, review of PBM compensation structures, audit authority, and rule promulgation. LDI reports that it otherwise does not possess the technical expertise necessary to provide adequate oversight and enforcement over PBMs. Note: Increased SGR expenditures within LDI results in less monies reverted to SGF at the end of the fiscal year. LDI reports that it can impose fees upon pharmacy benefit managers sufficient to provide for the costs detailed in this fiscal note (see NOTE in Revenue section below). EXPENDITURE EXPLANATION CONTINUED ON PAGE TWO Proposed law is anticipated to have an indeterminable impact on Statutory Dedication revenue within LDI. Proposed law provides for a civil monetary penalty not to exceed $1,000 per claim in addition to any other penalties authorized by law. The agency indicates that any fines or penalties collected pursuant to the proposed law are indeterminable. The LFO assumes fines will be deposited into the Pharmacy Benefits Manager Enforcement Fund in accordance with La R.S. 22:1870.1, and used first to provide for the expenses of the commissioner of insurance and the attorney general. NOTE: LDI reports that it possesses existing statutory authority under the provisions of La R.S. 22:1865(G), “to impose a reasonable fee upon pharmacy benefit managers, in accordance with the Administrative Procedure Act, in addition to a license fee and annual report fee, in order to cover the costs of implementation and enforcement,” of any legislation providing for oversight and enforcement duties with regard to PBMs. LDI has not promulgated any rules to this effect at the current time, and reports it anticipates being able to do so by the end of calendar year 2026. The LFO assumes fees associated with this effort would be categorized as SGR. REVENUE EXPLANATION EXPENDITURE EXPLANATION Provides relative to pharmacy benefit managers.
